effects_when_high: High sodium can cause fluid retention, high blood pressure, and in severe cases confusion and neurological symptoms, whereas low sodium can lead to nausea, headaches, muscle weakness, and in serious cases seizures or loss of consciousness.
effects_when_low: High sodium can cause fluid retention, high blood pressure, and in severe cases confusion and neurological symptoms, whereas low sodium can lead to nausea, headaches, muscle weakness, and in serious cases seizures or loss of consciousness.
what_to_do_when_low: Avoid excessive water intake and address any underlying hormonal or kidney-related cause with medical guidance to restore balance safely.
what_to_do_when_high: Increase fluid intake and reduce excess dietary salt, and consult a doctor if levels are significantly elevated or linked to an underlying condition.

meaning: Uric acid is a waste product formed during the breakdown of purines - compounds found naturally in certain foods and body cells and is normally filtered out by the kidneys through urine. Excess uric acid in the blood can accumulate in joints and tissues, contributing to gout and kidney stones.
causes_when_high: Purine-rich foods like organ meats and shellfish, fructose, alcohol, dehydration, poor kidney function, and metabolic syndrome can raise uric acid, while very low purine diets or certain medications can cause levels to drop below normal.
causes_when_low: Purine-rich foods like organ meats and shellfish, fructose, alcohol, dehydration, poor kidney function, and metabolic syndrome can raise uric acid, while very low purine diets or certain medications can cause levels to drop below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently high uric acid can lead to painful gout attacks, joint inflammation, and kidney stone formation, whereas low levels are generally not a concern and rarely cause noticeable symptoms.
effects_when_low: Persistently high uric acid can lead to painful gout attacks, joint inflammation, and kidney stone formation, whereas low levels are generally not a concern and rarely cause noticeable symptoms.
what_to_do_when_low: Low uric acid typically requires no intervention; maintaining a balanced diet is sufficient, though persistent low levels should be briefly reviewed with a doctor.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce intake of purine-rich foods, fructose, and alcohol, stay well hydrated to support kidney clearance, and consult a doctor if levels remain elevated or gout symptoms are present.

meaning: Triglycerides are a type of fat circulating in the blood, formed when the body converts excess calories it doesn't immediately need into stored fat. Persistently high levels can contribute to hardening and thickening of artery walls, significantly raising the risk of heart attack, stroke, and heart disease.
causes_when_high: Refined carbohydrates, sugar, fructose, alcohol, excess calorie intake, obesity, insulin resistance, and genetics are the primary drivers of elevated triglycerides, while very low fat diets or certain medications can push levels below normal.
causes_when_low: Refined carbohydrates, sugar, fructose, alcohol, excess calorie intake, obesity, insulin resistance, and genetics are the primary drivers of elevated triglycerides, while very low fat diets or certain medications can push levels below normal.
effects_when_high: High triglycerides accelerate arterial plaque buildup and increase cardiovascular risk, and at very high levels can also cause inflammation of the pancreas, whereas low levels are generally not a health concern.
effects_when_low: High triglycerides accelerate arterial plaque buildup and increase cardiovascular risk, and at very high levels can also cause inflammation of the pancreas, whereas low levels are generally not a health concern.
what_to_do_when_low: Low triglycerides require no intervention and are generally a positive sign of healthy fat metabolism and diet.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce refined carbs, sugar, and alcohol intake, incorporate omega-3 rich foods and regular exercise, and maintain a healthy weight - significant improvement can occur within 2–4 weeks of consistent changes.

meaning: Total cholesterol is a calculated sum of HDL, LDL, and VLDL levels in the blood, and serves as a broad initial indicator of cardiovascular health. It is also an essential building block for hormone production and vitamin D synthesis in the body.
causes_when_high: A diet high in saturated fat and refined carbohydrates, genetics, excess body weight, low physical activity, thyroid dysfunction, and certain medications can all raise total cholesterol, while very low fat diets or malnutrition can push levels below normal.
causes_when_low: A diet high in saturated fat and refined carbohydrates, genetics, excess body weight, low physical activity, thyroid dysfunction, and certain medications can all raise total cholesterol, while very low fat diets or malnutrition can push levels below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently high total cholesterol increases the risk of arterial plaque buildup and cardiovascular disease, though the balance between HDL and LDL matters more than the total number alone, whereas abnormally low levels may affect hormone production and overall cellular health.
effects_when_low: Persistently high total cholesterol increases the risk of arterial plaque buildup and cardiovascular disease, though the balance between HDL and LDL matters more than the total number alone, whereas abnormally low levels may affect hormone production and overall cellular health.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate intake of healthy fats from sources like nuts, seeds, and olive oil, and consult a doctor if levels are significantly low alongside other hormonal or nutritional concerns.
what_to_do_when_high: Focus on improving the HDL to LDL ratio through a whole foods diet, regular exercise, healthy weight maintenance, and limiting processed foods.

meaning: HDL is known as the "good cholesterol" because it transports cholesterol away from artery walls back to the liver for removal, actively helping prevent and reduce arterial plaque buildup. Higher HDL levels are consistently associated with a lower risk of heart disease and stroke.
causes_when_high: Regular exercise, healthy fat intake, and moderate lifestyle habits raise HDL, while smoking, trans fats, excessive refined carbohydrates, alcohol, and genetics can lower it below the protective range.
causes_when_low: Regular exercise, healthy fat intake, and moderate lifestyle habits raise HDL, while smoking, trans fats, excessive refined carbohydrates, alcohol, and genetics can lower it below the protective range.
effects_when_high: High HDL is protective and reduces cardiovascular risk by clearing excess cholesterol from arteries, whereas low HDL leaves arterial walls more vulnerable to plaque accumulation, increasing the long-term risk of heart disease.
effects_when_low: High HDL is protective and reduces cardiovascular risk by clearing excess cholesterol from arteries, whereas low HDL leaves arterial walls more vulnerable to plaque accumulation, increasing the long-term risk of heart disease.
what_to_do_when_low: Incorporate regular aerobic exercise, quit smoking, reduce trans fats and refined carbs, and add healthy fat sources like olive oil, nuts, and fatty fish
what_to_do_when_high: High HDL is a positive finding maintain the lifestyle habits supporting it, such as regular aerobic exercise and a diet rich in healthy fats.

meaning: Non-HDL cholesterol represents the total amount of "bad" cholesterol in the blood, calculated by subtracting HDL from total cholesterol. It is considered a more comprehensive and reliable marker of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone, as it captures all cholesterol-carrying particles that contribute to arterial plaque buildup.
causes_when_high: High saturated fat and refined carbohydrate intake, obesity, insulin resistance, low physical activity, genetics, and thyroid dysfunction can raise Non-HDL cholesterol, while very low fat diets or cholesterol-lowering medications can bring levels below normal.
causes_when_low: High saturated fat and refined carbohydrate intake, obesity, insulin resistance, low physical activity, genetics, and thyroid dysfunction can raise Non-HDL cholesterol, while very low fat diets or cholesterol-lowering medications can bring levels below normal.
effects_when_high: Elevated Non-HDL cholesterol accelerates plaque formation in artery walls, significantly increasing the risk of heart attack and stroke over time, whereas very low levels are uncommon and may occasionally affect hormone and cellular health.
effects_when_low: Elevated Non-HDL cholesterol accelerates plaque formation in artery walls, significantly increasing the risk of heart attack and stroke over time, whereas very low levels are uncommon and may occasionally affect hormone and cellular health.
what_to_do_when_low: Very low Non-HDL is rarely a concern; ensure adequate healthy fat intake and consult a doctor if it occurs alongside other significant lipid abnormalities.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce refined carbs, sugar, and saturated fats, prioritize fiber-rich whole foods, and maintain regular physical activity

meaning: The Cholesterol/HDL ratio is calculated by dividing total cholesterol by HDL, and is used as a quick indicator of cardiovascular risk. A lower ratio is better, as it reflects a healthier balance between total cholesterol and the protective HDL fraction.
causes_when_high: High total cholesterol, low HDL, poor diet, physical inactivity, smoking, and metabolic conditions drive the ratio higher, while regular exercise, healthy fat intake, and good overall lipid management bring it down.
causes_when_low: High total cholesterol, low HDL, poor diet, physical inactivity, smoking, and metabolic conditions drive the ratio higher, while regular exercise, healthy fat intake, and good overall lipid management bring it down.
effects_when_high: A high ratio signals a greater imbalance between harmful and protective cholesterol, increasing the risk of arterial plaque buildup and cardiovascular disease, whereas a low ratio reflects a favorable lipid profile with strong cardiovascular protection.
effects_when_low: A high ratio signals a greater imbalance between harmful and protective cholesterol, increasing the risk of arterial plaque buildup and cardiovascular disease, whereas a low ratio reflects a favorable lipid profile with strong cardiovascular protection.
what_to_do_when_low: A low ratio is a positive finding - maintain the diet and lifestyle habits that are supporting this healthy balance.
what_to_do_when_high: Focus on simultaneously lowering total cholesterol and raising HDL through regular aerobic exercise, a whole foods diet, and reducing refined carbs and unhealthy fats.

meaning: The HDL/LDL ratio is the inverse of the LDL/HDL ratio and measures how well the protective cholesterol balances against the harmful fraction. A higher ratio is better, indicating that HDL levels are strong relative to LDL, which is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk
causes_when_high: High HDL from regular exercise and healthy fat intake combined with well-managed LDL levels raises this ratio, while low HDL from smoking or inactivity and high LDL from poor diet and genetics can lower it significantly.
causes_when_low: High HDL from regular exercise and healthy fat intake combined with well-managed LDL levels raises this ratio, while low HDL from smoking or inactivity and high LDL from poor diet and genetics can lower it significantly.
effects_when_high: A high HDL/LDL ratio reflects strong cardiovascular protection with effective cholesterol clearance from arteries, whereas a low ratio indicates that harmful cholesterol is dominating, increasing the risk of plaque buildup and heart disease.
effects_when_low: A high HDL/LDL ratio reflects strong cardiovascular protection with effective cholesterol clearance from arteries, whereas a low ratio indicates that harmful cholesterol is dominating, increasing the risk of plaque buildup and heart disease.
what_to_do_when_low: Prioritize raising HDL through aerobic exercise and healthy fats while reducing LDL through fiber-rich foods, less processed food consumption, and avoiding smoking.
what_to_do_when_high: A high ratio is a positive sign - sustain regular physical activity, healthy fat consumption, and a balanced diet to maintain this favorable profile.

meaning: The LDL/HDL ratio compares the level of harmful cholesterol directly against the protective cholesterol, making it one of the most targeted indicators of cardiovascular risk. A lower ratio reflects better arterial protection and a healthier overall lipid balance.
causes_when_high: Elevated LDL from poor diet, inactivity, and genetics combined with low HDL from smoking, trans fat intake, and sedentary habits push this ratio higher, while lifestyle improvements that simultaneously lower LDL and raise HDL bring it down.
causes_when_low: Elevated LDL from poor diet, inactivity, and genetics combined with low HDL from smoking, trans fat intake, and sedentary habits push this ratio higher, while lifestyle improvements that simultaneously lower LDL and raise HDL bring it down.
effects_when_high: A high LDL/HDL ratio indicates that harmful cholesterol significantly outweighs the protective fraction, accelerating arterial plaque risk, whereas a low ratio reflects that HDL is effectively counterbalancing LDL's damaging effects.
effects_when_low: A high LDL/HDL ratio indicates that harmful cholesterol significantly outweighs the protective fraction, accelerating arterial plaque risk, whereas a low ratio reflects that HDL is effectively counterbalancing LDL's damaging effects.
what_to_do_when_low: A low ratio is desirable - continue the dietary and exercise habits that are keeping LDL in check and HDL levels healthy.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce LDL through fiber-rich foods and reduced saturated fat intake, while boosting HDL through regular aerobic exercise and healthy fat sources like nuts and olive oil.

meaning: Ferritin is a protein that stores iron in the body and releases it when needed. Blood ferritin levels act as the most reliable indicator of the body's total iron reserves. It is a key marker for diagnosing iron deficiency or iron overload conditions.
causes_when_high: Low ferritin is commonly caused by inadequate dietary iron intake, poor absorption, chronic blood loss (such as heavy periods or internal bleeding), or increased demand during pregnancy. High ferritin can result from excessive iron supplementation, frequent red meat consumption, liver disease, chronic inflammation, or conditions like haemochromatosis.
causes_when_low: Low ferritin is commonly caused by inadequate dietary iron intake, poor absorption, chronic blood loss (such as heavy periods or internal bleeding), or increased demand during pregnancy. High ferritin can result from excessive iron supplementation, frequent red meat consumption, liver disease, chronic inflammation, or conditions like haemochromatosis.
effects_when_high: Low ferritin leads to iron-deficiency anaemia, causing persistent fatigue, weakness, hair loss, and poor concentration. High ferritin over time can result in iron deposits in organs like the liver, heart, and joints, potentially leading to serious organ damage.
effects_when_low: Low ferritin leads to iron-deficiency anaemia, causing persistent fatigue, weakness, hair loss, and poor concentration. High ferritin over time can result in iron deposits in organs like the liver, heart, and joints, potentially leading to serious organ damage.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase consumption of iron-rich foods like red meat, lentils, and leafy greens, ideally paired with Vitamin C to boost absorption. A doctor may recommend iron supplementation depending on the severity.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce red meat and iron supplement intake, and consult a doctor to investigate underlying causes such as liver disease or haemochromatosis.

meaning: ALP is an enzyme found throughout the body, with the highest concentrations in the liver, bile ducts, and bones. It is primarily used as a marker to assess liver and bone health, and mildly elevated levels can be normal in growing children, teenagers, and during pregnancy.
causes_when_high: High ALP is commonly caused by bile duct obstruction, liver disease, bone disorders, or certain medications. Vitamin D deficiency can also contribute to elevated levels. Low ALP is less common but can be associated with nutritional deficiencies or rare metabolic conditions.
causes_when_low: High ALP is commonly caused by bile duct obstruction, liver disease, bone disorders, or certain medications. Vitamin D deficiency can also contribute to elevated levels. Low ALP is less common but can be associated with nutritional deficiencies or rare metabolic conditions.
effects_when_high: Persistently high ALP may reflect underlying liver dysfunction or abnormal bone turnover, and if left unaddressed, can indicate progressive damage to these organs. Low ALP, though rare, may point to poor nutrition or specific metabolic issues affecting bone and liver function.
effects_when_low: Persistently high ALP may reflect underlying liver dysfunction or abnormal bone turnover, and if left unaddressed, can indicate progressive damage to these organs. Low ALP, though rare, may point to poor nutrition or specific metabolic issues affecting bone and liver function.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate Vitamin D and overall nutritional intake. If levels are significantly low, seek medical evaluation to rule out any underlying metabolic condition.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to identify the underlying cause, whether liver or bone related. Addressing the root condition is the primary way to normalise ALP levels.

meaning: Free T4 is the primary hormone produced by the thyroid gland and serves as the main reserve that gets converted into the active Free T3 in the liver and tissues. It reflects how well the thyroid gland itself is functioning and is a key marker in evaluating overall thyroid health.
causes_when_high: Low Free T4 is commonly caused by an underactive thyroid, iodine deficiency, certain medications, or severe illness that suppresses thyroid gland function. High levels are typically associated with an overactive thyroid, excess iodine intake, or certain thyroid conditions that cause hormone overproduction.
causes_when_low: Low Free T4 is commonly caused by an underactive thyroid, iodine deficiency, certain medications, or severe illness that suppresses thyroid gland function. High levels are typically associated with an overactive thyroid, excess iodine intake, or certain thyroid conditions that cause hormone overproduction.
effects_when_high: Low Free T4 reduces the availability of active thyroid hormone in the body, leading to fatigue, weight gain, depression, and slowed bodily functions. High Free T4 overstimulates metabolism, causing symptoms like anxiety, rapid heartbeat, unexplained weight loss, and disrupted sleep.
effects_when_low: Low Free T4 reduces the availability of active thyroid hormone in the body, leading to fatigue, weight gain, depression, and slowed bodily functions. High Free T4 overstimulates metabolism, causing symptoms like anxiety, rapid heartbeat, unexplained weight loss, and disrupted sleep.
what_to_do_when_low: Seek medical evaluation to identify the underlying cause. Treatment typically involves thyroid hormone replacement therapy, and levels are monitored over weeks as changes are gradual.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor as high Free T4 usually requires medical treatment to manage thyroid overactivity or adjust existing thyroid medication.

meaning: Free T3 is the active form of thyroid hormone that directly controls how fast cells and tissues burn energy, making it a key marker of metabolic activity. It is converted from T4 (another thyroid hormone) primarily in the liver and kidneys, and reflects how well the thyroid system is functioning at a cellular level.
causes_when_high: Low Free T3 is often caused by severe illness, extreme calorie restriction, chronic stress, or poor selenium intake - all of which impair the conversion of T4 to T3. High levels are typically seen in an overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism) or from excess thyroid hormone supplementation.
causes_when_low: Low Free T3 is often caused by severe illness, extreme calorie restriction, chronic stress, or poor selenium intake - all of which impair the conversion of T4 to T3. High levels are typically seen in an overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism) or from excess thyroid hormone supplementation.
effects_when_high: Low Free T3 slows metabolism, leading to fatigue, weight gain, brain fog, and feeling constantly cold. High Free T3 speeds up metabolism excessively, causing symptoms like rapid heartbeat, weight loss, anxiety, and heat intolerance.
effects_when_low: Low Free T3 slows metabolism, leading to fatigue, weight gain, brain fog, and feeling constantly cold. High Free T3 speeds up metabolism excessively, causing symptoms like rapid heartbeat, weight loss, anxiety, and heat intolerance.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate calorie and carbohydrate intake, manage stress, and check selenium levels. A doctor should evaluate whether the low conversion is linked to an underlying illness or thyroid condition.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly, as high Free T3 may indicate hyperthyroidism and usually requires medical treatment or dosage adjustment if on thyroid medication.

meaning: LDH is an enzyme present in nearly all body tissues that gets released into the bloodstream when cells are damaged or destroyed. Elevated LDH is a general marker of tissue or muscle damage, and is used to assess the severity of various conditions rather than diagnose a specific one.
causes_when_high: High LDH is commonly triggered by intense physical exercise, muscle breakdown, hemolysis (destruction of red blood cells), liver disease, heart attack, or certain cancers. Low LDH is rare and generally not considered clinically significant. Note that improper sample handling can also produce falsely elevated results.
causes_when_low: High LDH is commonly triggered by intense physical exercise, muscle breakdown, hemolysis (destruction of red blood cells), liver disease, heart attack, or certain cancers. Low LDH is rare and generally not considered clinically significant. Note that improper sample handling can also produce falsely elevated results.
effects_when_high: Persistently high LDH signals ongoing tissue damage somewhere in the body, and the impact depends on the underlying cause - ranging from muscle soreness after exercise to more serious conditions affecting the liver, heart, or blood. Isolated or mildly elevated levels after exercise are usually harmless.
effects_when_low: Persistently high LDH signals ongoing tissue damage somewhere in the body, and the impact depends on the underlying cause - ranging from muscle soreness after exercise to more serious conditions affecting the liver, heart, or blood. Isolated or mildly elevated levels after exercise are usually harmless.
what_to_do_when_low: Low LDH does not typically require any action and is not associated with any known health concern.
what_to_do_when_high: Avoid intense exercise before retesting to rule out exercise-related elevation. If levels remain high at rest, consult a doctor to identify and treat the underlying cause.

meaning: Direct bilirubin is the water-soluble form of bilirubin that has been processed by the liver and is ready for elimination from the body. Elevated levels specifically point to a problem with bile flow or liver processing, making it a more targeted marker for liver and bile duct health compared to total bilirubin.
causes_when_high: High direct bilirubin is primarily caused by bile duct obstruction, liver disease that impairs bile flow, or certain medications. Unlike indirect bilirubin, it is not elevated by hemolysis (red blood cell breakdown) alone, which helps narrow down the cause. Low direct bilirubin is generally not clinically significant.
causes_when_low: High direct bilirubin is primarily caused by bile duct obstruction, liver disease that impairs bile flow, or certain medications. Unlike indirect bilirubin, it is not elevated by hemolysis (red blood cell breakdown) alone, which helps narrow down the cause. Low direct bilirubin is generally not clinically significant.
effects_when_high: Elevated direct bilirubin can cause jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), dark urine, and pale stools, all of which indicate impaired bile elimination. If the underlying cause is left untreated, it can lead to progressive liver damage or complications from bile duct obstruction.
effects_when_low: Elevated direct bilirubin can cause jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes), dark urine, and pale stools, all of which indicate impaired bile elimination. If the underlying cause is left untreated, it can lead to progressive liver damage or complications from bile duct obstruction.
what_to_do_when_low: Low direct bilirubin requires no specific action and is not associated with any known health concern.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly to investigate for liver disease or bile duct obstruction. If a medication is suspected, the doctor may review or adjust it, with levels potentially resolving within weeks of stopping the drug.

meaning: SGPT, also known as ALT, is an enzyme found primarily in the liver. It is one of the most direct and specific markers of liver cell health - when liver cells are damaged or inflamed, SGPT leaks into the bloodstream, causing levels to rise. It is routinely tested to assess liver function and detect liver disease early.
causes_when_high: High SGPT is commonly caused by fatty liver disease, alcohol consumption, viral hepatitis, certain medications, or obesity and insulin resistance. Intense exercise can also cause a mild temporary rise. Low SGPT is uncommon and generally not considered medically significant.
causes_when_low: High SGPT is commonly caused by fatty liver disease, alcohol consumption, viral hepatitis, certain medications, or obesity and insulin resistance. Intense exercise can also cause a mild temporary rise. Low SGPT is uncommon and generally not considered medically significant.
effects_when_high: Persistently elevated SGPT indicates ongoing liver cell damage which, if unaddressed, can progress to chronic liver disease, fibrosis, or cirrhosis over time. Mild elevations are often symptom-free, making routine testing important for early detection.
effects_when_low: Persistently elevated SGPT indicates ongoing liver cell damage which, if unaddressed, can progress to chronic liver disease, fibrosis, or cirrhosis over time. Mild elevations are often symptom-free, making routine testing important for early detection.
what_to_do_when_low: Low SGPT does not require any specific action and is not associated with any known health concern.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce or eliminate alcohol, avoid unnecessary medications or supplements that stress the liver, and consult a doctor to identify the root cause. Lifestyle changes like weight loss and a balanced diet can significantly help in cases of fatty liver.

meaning: SGOT, also known as AST, is an enzyme found primarily in the liver, heart, and muscles. It is released into the bloodstream when any of these tissues are damaged or under stress. While it is used as a liver health marker similar to SGPT, it is less liver-specific since it can also rise due to heart or muscle injury.
causes_when_high: High SGOT can be caused by liver disease, fatty liver, alcohol consumption, intense exercise, muscle injury, heart conditions, or certain medications. Because it is present in multiple organs, an elevated result often needs to be interpreted alongside other markers like SGPT and CPK to pinpoint the source. Low SGOT is generally not clinically significant.
causes_when_low: High SGOT can be caused by liver disease, fatty liver, alcohol consumption, intense exercise, muscle injury, heart conditions, or certain medications. Because it is present in multiple organs, an elevated result often needs to be interpreted alongside other markers like SGPT and CPK to pinpoint the source. Low SGOT is generally not clinically significant.
effects_when_high: Persistently high SGOT reflects ongoing damage to the liver, heart, or muscles, and if left unaddressed, can indicate or contribute to progressive organ disease. Mild, short-lived elevations after exercise are harmless and self-resolving.
effects_when_low: Persistently high SGOT reflects ongoing damage to the liver, heart, or muscles, and if left unaddressed, can indicate or contribute to progressive organ disease. Mild, short-lived elevations after exercise are harmless and self-resolving.
what_to_do_when_low: Low SGOT does not require any specific action and is not associated with any known health concern
what_to_do_when_high: Limit alcohol, avoid unnecessary medications, and manage weight through diet and exercise. Consult a doctor if levels remain elevated at rest, as further investigation may be needed to identify the affected organ.

meaning: Total bilirubin measures the overall level of bilirubin in the blood - a yellow compound produced when red blood cells are broken down and recycled. The liver processes and eliminates it from the body. It serves as a broad marker of both liver function and red blood cell turnover, and elevated levels are a common cause of jaundice.
causes_when_high: High total bilirubin can result from liver disease, bile duct obstruction, or hemolysis (excessive red blood cell breakdown). Gilbert syndrome, a harmless genetic condition affecting around 5% of people, can also cause mildly elevated levels. Fasting can temporarily raise bilirubin as well. Low total bilirubin is generally not clinically significant.
causes_when_low: High total bilirubin can result from liver disease, bile duct obstruction, or hemolysis (excessive red blood cell breakdown). Gilbert syndrome, a harmless genetic condition affecting around 5% of people, can also cause mildly elevated levels. Fasting can temporarily raise bilirubin as well. Low total bilirubin is generally not clinically significant.
effects_when_high: Elevated bilirubin causes jaundice - visible yellowing of the skin and eyes - along with dark urine and fatigue. If driven by an underlying liver or bile duct condition, persistently high levels can indicate or worsen organ damage over time. Gilbert syndrome-related elevation is benign and requires no treatment.
effects_when_low: Elevated bilirubin causes jaundice - visible yellowing of the skin and eyes - along with dark urine and fatigue. If driven by an underlying liver or bile duct condition, persistently high levels can indicate or worsen organ damage over time. Gilbert syndrome-related elevation is benign and requires no treatment.
what_to_do_when_low: Low total bilirubin does not require any specific action and is not associated with any known health concern.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to determine the underlying cause. Avoid alcohol and hepatotoxic substances, and maintain a healthy weight to support liver health. If fasting-related, eating regularly can help normalise levels quickly.

meaning: Indirect bilirubin is the unprocessed form of bilirubin that is yet to be taken up and converted by the liver. Measuring it helps distinguish whether elevated bilirubin is coming from excessive red blood cell breakdown or from impaired liver uptake, making it a useful marker for identifying the source of the problem.
causes_when_high: High indirect bilirubin is primarily caused by hemolysis (increased red blood cell destruction), impaired liver uptake, or Gilbert syndrome - a common benign genetic condition that causes mild fluctuations, especially with fasting or stress. Low indirect bilirubin is generally not clinically significant.
causes_when_low: High indirect bilirubin is primarily caused by hemolysis (increased red blood cell destruction), impaired liver uptake, or Gilbert syndrome - a common benign genetic condition that causes mild fluctuations, especially with fasting or stress. Low indirect bilirubin is generally not clinically significant.
effects_when_high: Elevated indirect bilirubin can cause jaundice and, if driven by significant hemolysis, may lead to anaemia and related symptoms like fatigue and weakness over time. Gilbert syndrome-related elevation is harmless and does not cause any lasting health effects.
effects_when_low: Elevated indirect bilirubin can cause jaundice and, if driven by significant hemolysis, may lead to anaemia and related symptoms like fatigue and weakness over time. Gilbert syndrome-related elevation is harmless and does not cause any lasting health effects.
what_to_do_when_low: Low indirect bilirubin does not require any specific action and is not associated with any known health concern.
what_to_do_when_high: if Gilbert syndrome is the likely cause, no treatment is needed. Avoid prolonged fasting to minimise fluctuations. If hemolysis is suspected, consult a doctor to identify and address the underlying condition.

meaning: Albumin is the most abundant protein in the blood, produced by the liver. It plays a key role in maintaining fluid balance between blood vessels and tissues, and serves as the body's primary transport protein for hormones, medications, and nutrients. It is a reliable marker of both liver function and overall nutritional status.
causes_when_high: Low albumin is commonly caused by liver disease, malnutrition, chronic inflammation, or protein-losing conditions such as kidney disease. High albumin is rare and is most often a result of dehydration rather than any underlying disease.
causes_when_low: Low albumin is commonly caused by liver disease, malnutrition, chronic inflammation, or protein-losing conditions such as kidney disease. High albumin is rare and is most often a result of dehydration rather than any underlying disease.
effects_when_high: Low albumin can cause fluid to leak from blood vessels into surrounding tissues, leading to swelling particularly in the legs and abdomen. It also impairs the transport of essential substances in the blood, and is generally a sign of significant nutritional deficiency or organ dysfunction. High albumin due to dehydration typically resolves once fluid intake is restored.
effects_when_low: Low albumin can cause fluid to leak from blood vessels into surrounding tissues, leading to swelling particularly in the legs and abdomen. It also impairs the transport of essential substances in the blood, and is generally a sign of significant nutritional deficiency or organ dysfunction. High albumin due to dehydration typically resolves once fluid intake is restored.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate protein intake through diet, focusing on quality sources like eggs, meat, legumes, and dairy. Consult a doctor to identify and address the underlying cause, whether nutritional, hepatic, or renal in origin.
what_to_do_when_high: Increase fluid intake to rule out dehydration as the cause. If levels remain high after adequate hydration, consult a doctor for further evaluation.

meaning: The A/G ratio compares the levels of albumin to globulin in the blood. Rather than measuring a single protein, it reflects the balance between the two, offering a more nuanced view of liver function, immune activity, and overall protein health. An abnormal ratio often prompts further investigation even when individual protein levels appear borderline.
causes_when_high: A low A/G ratio is commonly caused by liver disease, chronic infections, autoimmune conditions, or kidney disorders situations where albumin drops and/or globulin rises. A high A/G ratio is less common and may be seen in certain immune deficiencies or genetic conditions where globulin production is reduced.
causes_when_low: A low A/G ratio is commonly caused by liver disease, chronic infections, autoimmune conditions, or kidney disorders situations where albumin drops and/or globulin rises. A high A/G ratio is less common and may be seen in certain immune deficiencies or genetic conditions where globulin production is reduced.
effects_when_high: A low ratio suggests the balance has shifted away from functional proteins toward immune or inflammatory proteins, which can indicate chronic disease burden on the liver or immune system. A persistently high ratio may reflect impaired immune protein production, weakening the body's defence mechanisms.
effects_when_low: A low ratio suggests the balance has shifted away from functional proteins toward immune or inflammatory proteins, which can indicate chronic disease burden on the liver or immune system. A persistently high ratio may reflect impaired immune protein production, weakening the body's defence mechanisms.
what_to_do_when_low: Seek medical evaluation to identify whether the imbalance is driven by liver disease, chronic inflammation, or protein loss. Addressing the root cause is the primary way to restore a healthy ratio.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor if significantly elevated, as it may indicate an underlying immune deficiency. Ensure adequate overall nutrition to support healthy protein production.

meaning: Average Blood Glucose (eAG) is a value derived directly from HbA1c, translating the percentage into an estimated average blood sugar level in familiar units (mg/dL or mmol/L). It makes long-term blood sugar data easier to understand and compare against daily glucose readings for a more intuitive picture of overall sugar control.
causes_when_high: Since eAG is calculated from HbA1c, the same factors apply - high sugar and refined carb intake, inactivity, poor sleep, and insulin resistance raise it, while effective lifestyle management or blood sugar-lowering treatment bring it down.
causes_when_low: Since eAG is calculated from HbA1c, the same factors apply - high sugar and refined carb intake, inactivity, poor sleep, and insulin resistance raise it, while effective lifestyle management or blood sugar-lowering treatment bring it down.
effects_when_high: A persistently high eAG reflects the same long-term risks as elevated HbA1c - progressive damage to blood vessels, nerves, and organs - whereas a low eAG may indicate over-treatment or overly aggressive dietary restriction of carbohydrates.
effects_when_low: A persistently high eAG reflects the same long-term risks as elevated HbA1c - progressive damage to blood vessels, nerves, and organs - whereas a low eAG may indicate over-treatment or overly aggressive dietary restriction of carbohydrates.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate carbohydrate intake for stable energy and consult a doctor if the low reading is associated with blood sugar-lowering medications that may need to be reviewed.
what_to_do_when_high: Follow the same approach as HbA1c management - reduce sugar and refined carb intake, increase physical activity, improve sleep, and consult a doctor for further evaluation if levels remain in a concerning range.

meaning: Serum total protein measures the combined level of all proteins in the blood, primarily albumin and globulins. It reflects the body's overall protein status and gives a broad overview of liver function, immune health, and nutritional adequacy. It is often used as a starting point to investigate further with albumin and globulin individually.
causes_when_high: Low total protein is commonly caused by malnutrition, liver disease, kidney disorders that cause protein loss, or chronic inflammatory conditions that impair protein synthesis. High total protein is typically associated with dehydration or conditions that cause excessive immune protein production, such as chronic infections or certain blood disorders.
causes_when_low: Low total protein is commonly caused by malnutrition, liver disease, kidney disorders that cause protein loss, or chronic inflammatory conditions that impair protein synthesis. High total protein is typically associated with dehydration or conditions that cause excessive immune protein production, such as chronic infections or certain blood disorders.
effects_when_high: Low total protein can lead to fluid imbalance, tissue swelling, weakened immunity, and poor wound healing, reflecting an overall deficit in the body's essential building blocks. Persistently high total protein may indicate an underlying inflammatory or immune condition that warrants further investigation.
effects_when_low: Low total protein can lead to fluid imbalance, tissue swelling, weakened immunity, and poor wound healing, reflecting an overall deficit in the body's essential building blocks. Persistently high total protein may indicate an underlying inflammatory or immune condition that warrants further investigation.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase dietary protein intake through foods like eggs, meat, fish, legumes, and dairy. Seek medical evaluation to identify whether the cause is nutritional, liver-related, or due to excess protein loss through the kidneys.
what_to_do_when_high: Ensure adequate hydration and consult a doctor if levels remain elevated, as persistently high protein may need further testing to rule out underlying blood or immune conditions.

meaning: Phosphorus is an essential mineral found mostly in bones and teeth, with a smaller amount circulating in the blood where it plays a key role in energy production, cell repair, and maintaining the body's acid-base balance. Blood phosphorus levels are closely regulated by the kidneys, parathyroid hormone, and vitamin D, making it a useful marker for assessing bone, kidney, and hormonal health.
causes_when_high: Poor kidney function, hypoparathyroidism, excess phosphorus intake, and vitamin D toxicity can raise levels, while malnutrition, vitamin D deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, and certain medications like antacids can deplete phosphorus below normal.
causes_when_low: Poor kidney function, hypoparathyroidism, excess phosphorus intake, and vitamin D toxicity can raise levels, while malnutrition, vitamin D deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, and certain medications like antacids can deplete phosphorus below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently high phosphorus can disrupt calcium balance, weaken bones, and strain blood vessels over time, whereas low phosphorus can cause muscle weakness, bone pain, fatigue, and impaired energy production at the cellular level.
effects_when_low: Persistently high phosphorus can disrupt calcium balance, weaken bones, and strain blood vessels over time, whereas low phosphorus can cause muscle weakness, bone pain, fatigue, and impaired energy production at the cellular level.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate intake of phosphorus-rich foods such as dairy, eggs, meat, and legumes, and address any underlying vitamin D deficiency or medication-related cause under medical guidance.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to evaluate kidney function and parathyroid hormone levels, and reduce intake of high-phosphorus processed foods and supplements if identified as a contributing factor.

meaning: The BUN/Creatinine Ratio compares blood urea nitrogen to creatinine levels to help pinpoint the location and cause of kidney dysfunction. It is a diagnostic tool that helps distinguish between dehydration or reduced blood flow to the kidneys versus direct kidney tissue damage, making it more informative than either marker alone.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, high protein intake, gastrointestinal bleeding, heart failure, and reduced kidney blood flow raise the ratio, while low protein intake, liver disease, or conditions causing isolated creatinine elevation such as high muscle mass or acute kidney damage can lower it.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, high protein intake, gastrointestinal bleeding, heart failure, and reduced kidney blood flow raise the ratio, while low protein intake, liver disease, or conditions causing isolated creatinine elevation such as high muscle mass or acute kidney damage can lower it.
effects_when_high: A high ratio suggests the kidneys are receiving insufficient blood flow or that excess nitrogen is being produced, whereas a low ratio may indicate direct kidney tissue damage or inadequate protein intake affecting urea production.
effects_when_low: A high ratio suggests the kidneys are receiving insufficient blood flow or that excess nitrogen is being produced, whereas a low ratio may indicate direct kidney tissue damage or inadequate protein intake affecting urea production.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor to evaluate for direct kidney damage or liver dysfunction, as a persistently low ratio alongside other abnormal markers warrants further investigation.
what_to_do_when_high: Improve hydration, moderate protein intake, and consult a doctor to rule out gastrointestinal bleeding or reduced cardiac output as contributing causes.

meaning: The Urea/Creatinine Ratio serves the same diagnostic purpose as the BUN/Creatinine Ratio but uses total blood urea rather than blood urea nitrogen in the calculation. It is commonly used in regions where urea is the standard reported metric, and helps differentiate between pre-renal causes of kidney stress and intrinsic kidney damage.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, high protein diet, gastrointestinal bleeding, and reduced kidney blood flow from heart failure or volume depletion raise the ratio, while low protein intake, liver disease, or direct kidney tissue injury causing disproportionate creatinine rise can lower it.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, high protein diet, gastrointestinal bleeding, and reduced kidney blood flow from heart failure or volume depletion raise the ratio, while low protein intake, liver disease, or direct kidney tissue injury causing disproportionate creatinine rise can lower it.
effects_when_high: A high ratio points to insufficient kidney perfusion or excess urea production rather than structural kidney damage, whereas a low ratio suggests the kidneys themselves may be structurally compromised, impairing their ability to filter creatinine effectively.
effects_when_low: A high ratio points to insufficient kidney perfusion or excess urea production rather than structural kidney damage, whereas a low ratio suggests the kidneys themselves may be structurally compromised, impairing their ability to filter creatinine effectively.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor for a thorough kidney and liver function assessment, as a persistently low ratio may reflect underlying structural damage requiring targeted medical management.
what_to_do_when_high: Prioritize adequate hydration and moderate protein consumption, and seek medical evaluation if gastrointestinal bleeding or heart-related causes are suspected.

meaning: Vitamin B12 is a water-soluble vitamin essential for red blood cell production, DNA repair, and brain and nervous system health. Since the body cannot produce it on its own, it must be obtained entirely through diet or supplements.
causes_when_high: Low B12 is commonly seen in vegetarians, vegans, older adults, or those on medications like metformin or PPIs, as absorption requires stomach acid and a protein called intrinsic factor. High B12 levels are typically linked to excess supplementation or certain underlying health conditions.
causes_when_low: Low B12 is commonly seen in vegetarians, vegans, older adults, or those on medications like metformin or PPIs, as absorption requires stomach acid and a protein called intrinsic factor. High B12 levels are typically linked to excess supplementation or certain underlying health conditions.
effects_when_high: Low B12 can cause anaemia, fatigue, nerve damage, and cognitive issues, with neurological effects being harder to reverse if deficiency is prolonged. Elevated levels are generally less concerning but may occasionally signal liver or blood disorders.
effects_when_low: Low B12 can cause anaemia, fatigue, nerve damage, and cognitive issues, with neurological effects being harder to reverse if deficiency is prolonged. Elevated levels are generally less concerning but may occasionally signal liver or blood disorders.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase intake of animal-based foods like meat, fish, eggs, or dairy. Vegetarians and vegans should consider supplementation - sublingual or injectable forms work best if absorption is an issue. Levels can rise within weeks with consistent supplementation.
what_to_do_when_high: Review your current supplement intake and consult a doctor to rule out any underlying condition.

meaning: Metabolic syndrome is not a single disease but a cluster of interconnected metabolic abnormalities — including high blood sugar, excess abdominal fat, high blood pressure, high triglycerides, and low HDL cholesterol — that together significantly raise the risk of type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and stroke. It is assessed as a combined marker of overall metabolic health, and the presence of three or more of these conditions together is typically used to confirm the diagnosis.
causes_when_high: A sedentary lifestyle, poor diet high in refined carbohydrates and processed foods, excess visceral fat, chronic stress, poor sleep, insulin resistance, hormonal imbalances, and genetic predisposition are the primary drivers of metabolic syndrome, while an active lifestyle, balanced nutrition, and healthy body weight significantly reduce the likelihood of developing it.
causes_when_low: A sedentary lifestyle, poor diet high in refined carbohydrates and processed foods, excess visceral fat, chronic stress, poor sleep, insulin resistance, hormonal imbalances, and genetic predisposition are the primary drivers of metabolic syndrome, while an active lifestyle, balanced nutrition, and healthy body weight significantly reduce the likelihood of developing it.
effects_when_high: Unmanaged metabolic syndrome progressively increases the risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, fatty liver disease, kidney damage, and hormonal disruption, whereas the absence of metabolic syndrome reflects good overall metabolic health with a significantly lower risk of chronic disease development over time.
effects_when_low: Unmanaged metabolic syndrome progressively increases the risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, fatty liver disease, kidney damage, and hormonal disruption, whereas the absence of metabolic syndrome reflects good overall metabolic health with a significantly lower risk of chronic disease development over time.
what_to_do_when_low: The absence of metabolic syndrome is a positive finding — maintain the healthy lifestyle habits supporting good metabolic health including regular physical activity, balanced nutrition, adequate sleep, and effective stress management to prevent its development over time.
what_to_do_when_high: Address all contributing factors simultaneously — reduce refined carbohydrate and processed food intake, incorporate regular aerobic and resistance exercise, achieve and maintain a healthy body weight, improve sleep quality, and manage chronic stress, as meaningful improvement across all markers can be seen within 8–12 weeks of consistent lifestyle changes.

meaning: Hypertension is a chronic condition characterized by persistently elevated pressure of blood against the artery walls, placing continuous strain on the heart, blood vessels, and vital organs over time. It is often referred to as the "silent killer" as it rarely causes noticeable symptoms in its early stages yet significantly increases the risk of heart attack, stroke, and organ damage if left unmanaged.
causes_when_high: Excess dietary salt, obesity, physical inactivity, chronic stress, poor sleep, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, genetic predisposition, kidney disease, and hormonal disorders can all drive blood pressure higher, while regular physical activity, a balanced low-sodium diet, healthy body weight, and effective stress management support healthy blood pressure levels.
causes_when_low: Excess dietary salt, obesity, physical inactivity, chronic stress, poor sleep, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, genetic predisposition, kidney disease, and hormonal disorders can all drive blood pressure higher, while regular physical activity, a balanced low-sodium diet, healthy body weight, and effective stress management support healthy blood pressure levels.
effects_when_high: Persistently high blood pressure progressively damages blood vessel walls, significantly increasing the risk of heart attack, stroke, kidney disease, vision loss, and heart failure over time, whereas well-controlled blood pressure protects vital organs, reduces cardiovascular risk, and supports better long-term health outcomes.
effects_when_low: Persistently high blood pressure progressively damages blood vessel walls, significantly increasing the risk of heart attack, stroke, kidney disease, vision loss, and heart failure over time, whereas well-controlled blood pressure protects vital organs, reduces cardiovascular risk, and supports better long-term health outcomes.
what_to_do_when_low: Healthy or well-controlled blood pressure is a positive finding — maintain regular physical activity, a balanced low-sodium diet, healthy body weight, limited alcohol intake, and effective stress management to sustain healthy blood pressure levels long term.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce dietary salt and processed food intake, incorporate regular aerobic exercise, achieve and maintain a healthy body weight, limit alcohol consumption, quit smoking, and prioritize quality sleep and stress management — consult a doctor for a comprehensive management plan that may include medication alongside lifestyle changes for significant and sustained improvement.

meaning: Cardiac health refers to the overall functional state of the heart and cardiovascular system, encompassing the heart's ability to pump blood efficiently, maintain healthy blood pressure, and sustain adequate circulation to all organs and tissues. It is assessed through a combination of markers including blood pressure, cholesterol levels, inflammatory markers, blood sugar, and lifestyle factors, making it a broad but critical indicator of long-term health and longevity.
causes_when_high: Poor diet high in saturated fats, refined carbohydrates, and excess sodium, physical inactivity, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, chronic stress, poor sleep, obesity, uncontrolled blood sugar, high blood pressure, and genetic predisposition are the primary drivers of poor cardiac health, while regular physical activity, a balanced diet, healthy body weight, and effective stress management support a strong and healthy cardiovascular system.
causes_when_low: Poor diet high in saturated fats, refined carbohydrates, and excess sodium, physical inactivity, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, chronic stress, poor sleep, obesity, uncontrolled blood sugar, high blood pressure, and genetic predisposition are the primary drivers of poor cardiac health, while regular physical activity, a balanced diet, healthy body weight, and effective stress management support a strong and healthy cardiovascular system.
effects_when_high: Poor cardiac health significantly increases the risk of heart attack, stroke, heart failure, arterial plaque buildup, irregular heart rhythms, and cardiovascular-related organ damage over time, whereas good cardiac health supports efficient circulation, stable energy levels, healthy blood pressure, and a significantly reduced risk of life-threatening cardiovascular events.
effects_when_low: Poor cardiac health significantly increases the risk of heart attack, stroke, heart failure, arterial plaque buildup, irregular heart rhythms, and cardiovascular-related organ damage over time, whereas good cardiac health supports efficient circulation, stable energy levels, healthy blood pressure, and a significantly reduced risk of life-threatening cardiovascular events.
what_to_do_when_low: Good cardiac health is a positive finding — maintain regular aerobic exercise, a heart-healthy diet rich in omega-3s, fiber, and antioxidants, healthy body weight, limited alcohol intake, adequate sleep, and effective stress management to sustain long term cardiovascular health and reduce future risk.
what_to_do_when_high: Address all modifiable risk factors simultaneously — reduce saturated fat, sodium, and refined carbohydrate intake, incorporate regular aerobic exercise, quit smoking, limit alcohol, manage chronic stress, control blood sugar and blood pressure, and consult a doctor for a comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment and personalized management plan that may include medications alongside lifestyle changes for meaningful and sustained improvement.

meaning: CPK is an enzyme found primarily in the heart, brain, and skeletal muscles that leaks into the bloodstream when these tissues are injured or under stress. It is most commonly used as a marker of muscle damage, though elevated levels can also point to heart or brain injury depending on the context.
causes_when_high: High CPK is most frequently caused by intense exercise, muscle trauma, injections, or seizures. Statins and certain other medications can also damage muscle tissue and raise CPK. A heart attack releases a cardiac-specific form of CPK, making it a useful marker in that context as well. Low CPK is uncommon and rarely a clinical concern.
causes_when_low: High CPK is most frequently caused by intense exercise, muscle trauma, injections, or seizures. Statins and certain other medications can also damage muscle tissue and raise CPK. A heart attack releases a cardiac-specific form of CPK, making it a useful marker in that context as well. Low CPK is uncommon and rarely a clinical concern.
effects_when_high: Elevated CPK indicates active muscle or tissue breakdown, which can cause muscle pain, weakness, and in severe cases, lead to a condition called rhabdomyolysis where large-scale muscle damage strains the kidneys. Mild post-exercise elevation is normal and self-resolving.
effects_when_low: Elevated CPK indicates active muscle or tissue breakdown, which can cause muscle pain, weakness, and in severe cases, lead to a condition called rhabdomyolysis where large-scale muscle damage strains the kidneys. Mild post-exercise elevation is normal and self-resolving.
what_to_do_when_low: Low CPK is generally not a concern and does not require any specific action.
what_to_do_when_high: Rest and avoid intense physical activity -  exercise-induced elevation typically peaks within 24–72 hours and normalises within 3–5 days. If elevation persists without an obvious cause, consult a doctor to investigate further.

meaning: Hs-CRP is a protein produced by the liver in response to inflammation, and its levels directly reflect how much inflammation is present in the body. It is one of the most sensitive and widely used markers for detecting both acute infections and chronic low-grade inflammation. Higher values consistently point to a greater inflammatory burden on the body.
causes_when_high: Acute infections, injuries, obesity, poor diet, lack of exercise, disrupted sleep, and chronic stress can all raise Hs-CRP levels, while genetics also play a contributing role in baseline inflammation levels.
causes_when_low: Acute infections, injuries, obesity, poor diet, lack of exercise, disrupted sleep, and chronic stress can all raise Hs-CRP levels, while genetics also play a contributing role in baseline inflammation levels.
effects_when_high: Elevated Hs-CRP signals active inflammation that, if persistent, can increase the risk of cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, and immune dysfunction, whereas low levels reflect a well-regulated inflammatory response and good overall health.
effects_when_low: Elevated Hs-CRP signals active inflammation that, if persistent, can increase the risk of cardiovascular disease, metabolic disorders, and immune dysfunction, whereas low levels reflect a well-regulated inflammatory response and good overall health.
what_to_do_when_low: Low Hs-CRP is a positive indicator and requires no intervention. Continuing with a balanced lifestyle that supports low inflammation is all that is needed to maintain healthy levels.
what_to_do_when_high: Adopt an anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3s and vegetables, exercise regularly, maintain a healthy weight, and prioritize quality sleep and stress management. Acute elevations from infections typically resolve within 2–4 weeks, while lifestyle-driven elevations improve over 2–3 months.

meaning: ESR is a blood test that indirectly measures inflammation by tracking how quickly red blood cells settle to the bottom of a test tube. When inflammation is present, red blood cells clump together and sink faster, resulting in a higher reading.
causes_when_high: Inflammation, infections, autoimmune conditions, cancer, and anemia can all raise ESR, while age and pregnancy naturally elevate it as well. Certain medications can also influence readings in either direction.
causes_when_low: Inflammation, infections, autoimmune conditions, cancer, and anemia can all raise ESR, while age and pregnancy naturally elevate it as well. Certain medications can also influence readings in either direction.
effects_when_high: A persistently elevated ESR signals ongoing inflammation or an underlying condition that the body is actively responding to, whereas a low ESR is generally not a concern and typically reflects the absence of significant inflammatory activity.
effects_when_low: A persistently elevated ESR signals ongoing inflammation or an underlying condition that the body is actively responding to, whereas a low ESR is generally not a concern and typically reflects the absence of significant inflammatory activity.
what_to_do_when_low: A low ESR requires no intervention and is generally a reassuring finding. Maintaining an overall healthy and anti-inflammatory lifestyle is sufficient to keep levels stable.
what_to_do_when_high: A persistently elevated ESR signals ongoing inflammation or an underlying condition that the body is actively responding to, whereas a low ESR is generally not a concern and typically reflects the absence of significant inflammatory activity.

meaning: Zinc is an essential trace mineral critical for immune function, wound healing, DNA synthesis, cell division, and the proper functioning of over 300 enzymes in the body. It is not stored in large amounts, making regular dietary intake important, and blood zinc levels serve as a useful but imperfect indicator of overall zinc status.
causes_when_high: Excess supplementation or occupational zinc exposure can raise levels, while poor dietary intake, vegetarian or vegan diets low in bioavailable zinc, digestive disorders, chronic illness, and certain medications that impair absorption are the most common drivers of deficiency.
causes_when_low: Excess supplementation or occupational zinc exposure can raise levels, while poor dietary intake, vegetarian or vegan diets low in bioavailable zinc, digestive disorders, chronic illness, and certain medications that impair absorption are the most common drivers of deficiency.
effects_when_high: Excess zinc can interfere with copper absorption, impair immune function, and cause nausea and digestive discomfort, whereas low zinc weakens immune defense, slows wound healing, impairs taste and smell, and can negatively affect hormonal health and cognitive function.
effects_when_low: Excess zinc can interfere with copper absorption, impair immune function, and cause nausea and digestive discomfort, whereas low zinc weakens immune defense, slows wound healing, impairs taste and smell, and can negatively affect hormonal health and cognitive function.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase intake of zinc-rich foods such as meat, shellfish, legumes, nuts, and seeds, and consider a zinc supplement under medical guidance, particularly if absorption issues or dietary restrictions are the underlying cause.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce zinc supplementation to avoid toxicity and consult a doctor if levels remain elevated, particularly to monitor copper status which is directly affected by excess zinc.

meaning: Magnesium is an essential mineral involved in over 300 biochemical processes in the body, including muscle and nerve function, blood sugar regulation, blood pressure control, and energy production. It is one of the most commonly deficient minerals, yet blood levels alone may not fully reflect total body stores as most magnesium is held within cells and bones.
causes_when_high: Kidney dysfunction and excess supplementation can raise magnesium levels, while poor dietary intake, excessive alcohol consumption, chronic stress, prolonged use of certain medications like diuretics or antacids, and digestive disorders impairing absorption commonly deplete it.
causes_when_low: Kidney dysfunction and excess supplementation can raise magnesium levels, while poor dietary intake, excessive alcohol consumption, chronic stress, prolonged use of certain medications like diuretics or antacids, and digestive disorders impairing absorption commonly deplete it.
effects_when_high: Excess magnesium can cause nausea, low blood pressure, muscle weakness, and in severe cases irregular heart rhythms, whereas low magnesium is associated with muscle cramps, poor sleep, anxiety, fatigue, and increased cardiovascular and metabolic risk over time.
effects_when_low: Excess magnesium can cause nausea, low blood pressure, muscle weakness, and in severe cases irregular heart rhythms, whereas low magnesium is associated with muscle cramps, poor sleep, anxiety, fatigue, and increased cardiovascular and metabolic risk over time.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase intake of magnesium-rich foods such as dark leafy greens, nuts, seeds, and whole grains, and consider supplementation under medical guidance if dietary correction alone is insufficient.
what_to_do_when_high: Discontinue excess supplementation and consult a doctor to evaluate kidney function, as healthy kidneys efficiently excrete excess magnesium under normal circumstances.

meaning: Absolute Neutrophil Count measures the exact number of neutrophils - the most abundant type of white blood cell - in your blood. These cells are your body's first line of defense against bacterial infections and inflammation. Low levels are a direct signal that the body may struggle to combat bacterial threats effectively.
causes_when_high: Bacterial infections and inflammation can push counts higher, while chemotherapy, certain medications, autoimmune conditions, or bone marrow disorders can cause levels to drop significantly.
causes_when_low: Bacterial infections and inflammation can push counts higher, while chemotherapy, certain medications, autoimmune conditions, or bone marrow disorders can cause levels to drop significantly.
effects_when_high: High counts typically reflect an active infection or inflammatory response the body is working to resolve, whereas low counts reduce the body's ability to fight bacteria, increasing the risk of serious infections.
effects_when_low: High counts typically reflect an active infection or inflammatory response the body is working to resolve, whereas low counts reduce the body's ability to fight bacteria, increasing the risk of serious infections.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor promptly, especially if linked to medications like chemotherapy. Supporting bone marrow health through a nutrient-rich diet particularly adequate B vitamins and protein can aid recovery over time.
what_to_do_when_high: Identify and treat the underlying infection or inflammation. Counts generally normalize on their own once the root cause is addressed, usually within days to weeks.

meaning: RBC Count measures the total number of red blood cells circulating in your blood. These cells are responsible for delivering oxygen to all tissues in the body and removing carbon dioxide as a waste product. This marker is a direct reflection of your body's capacity to meet its oxygen demands. Since red blood cells live for approximately 120 days, meaningful changes in this count tend to occur gradually over time.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, high-altitude living, and certain kidney or lung conditions can push RBC counts higher, while iron deficiency, low B12 or folate levels, poor bone marrow function, and chronic diseases can cause counts to fall below normal.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, high-altitude living, and certain kidney or lung conditions can push RBC counts higher, while iron deficiency, low B12 or folate levels, poor bone marrow function, and chronic diseases can cause counts to fall below normal.
effects_when_high: A high RBC count can thicken the blood, increasing the risk of clotting and circulation problems, whereas a low count reduces the body's oxygen-carrying capacity, leading to fatigue, breathlessness, and reduced physical and mental performance.
effects_when_low: A high RBC count can thicken the blood, increasing the risk of clotting and circulation problems, whereas a low count reduces the body's oxygen-carrying capacity, leading to fatigue, breathlessness, and reduced physical and mental performance.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate intake of iron, vitamin B12, and folate through diet or supplementation as advised by a doctor. Addressing any underlying conditions affecting bone marrow or kidney function is equally important. Improvement is gradual, typically appearing over 2–3 months.
what_to_do_when_high: Stay well hydrated to rule out dehydration as a contributing factor. If counts remain elevated, consult a doctor to check for underlying kidney, lung, or bone marrow conditions that may be driving excess red blood cell production

meaning: MPV measures the average size of platelets - the tiny cells responsible for blood clotting - circulating in your blood. Larger platelets are generally younger and more active, indicating that the bone marrow is producing them at a higher rate. This marker provides useful insight into platelet production activity and overall bone marrow function. It is best interpreted alongside the total platelet count for a more complete picture.
causes_when_high: High platelet destruction or turnover, inflammatory conditions, and certain blood disorders can cause the bone marrow to produce larger, younger platelets raising MPV, while conditions that suppress bone marrow activity or reduce platelet production can result in smaller, older platelets and a lower MPV
causes_when_low: High platelet destruction or turnover, inflammatory conditions, and certain blood disorders can cause the bone marrow to produce larger, younger platelets raising MPV, while conditions that suppress bone marrow activity or reduce platelet production can result in smaller, older platelets and a lower MPV
effects_when_high: A high MPV suggests the body is compensating for rapid platelet loss or destruction by producing more active platelets, which may also increase the risk of clotting, whereas a low MPV may indicate sluggish platelet production, potentially affecting the body's ability to clot properly.
effects_when_low: A high MPV suggests the body is compensating for rapid platelet loss or destruction by producing more active platelets, which may also increase the risk of clotting, whereas a low MPV may indicate sluggish platelet production, potentially affecting the body's ability to clot properly.
what_to_do_when_low: Seek medical evaluation to check for bone marrow suppression or conditions affecting platelet production. Treating the underlying cause typically helps normalize platelet size within 1–2 weeks as new platelets enter circulation.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to identify any underlying condition driving excess platelet turnover or inflammation. Since MPV is primarily a diagnostic indicator, treatment focuses on addressing the root cause rather than the value itself.

meaning: Absolute Monocyte Count measures the exact number of monocytes - a type of white blood cell - present in your blood. These cells play a vital role in fighting certain infections and clearing out damaged or dead cellular material from the body. They are also key players in regulating inflammation. Tracking this marker helps identify if the body is dealing with an ongoing infection or inflammatory condition.
causes_when_high: Chronic infections, inflammatory conditions, or the body's recovery phase can drive counts higher, while prolonged illness, certain bone marrow disorders, or immune suppression can cause them to drop below normal.
causes_when_low: Chronic infections, inflammatory conditions, or the body's recovery phase can drive counts higher, while prolonged illness, certain bone marrow disorders, or immune suppression can cause them to drop below normal.
effects_when_high: Elevated levels often signal that the body is actively responding to infection or inflammation, whereas low levels may indicate a weakened immune response, leaving the body less capable of clearing infections effectively.
effects_when_low: Elevated levels often signal that the body is actively responding to infection or inflammation, whereas low levels may indicate a weakened immune response, leaving the body less capable of clearing infections effectively.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor to rule out bone marrow issues or immune suppression. Supporting overall immune health through proper nutrition, rest, and managing any existing conditions is a good first step.
what_to_do_when_high: Focus on identifying and treating the underlying infection or inflammatory condition. Managing chronic inflammation through lifestyle changes and medical treatment can help bring levels back to normal over days to weeks.

meaning: Absolute Eosinophil Count measures the exact number of eosinophils - a type of white blood cell - circulating in your blood. These cells are primarily involved in the body's response to allergens and parasitic infections. Tracking this marker helps detect allergic conditions or infections that may otherwise go unnoticed.
causes_when_high: Allergic conditions, asthma, parasitic infections, or reactions to certain medications can cause eosinophil levels to rise. Elevated cortisol levels (due to stress or steroid use), alcohol intoxication, or certain acute infections can suppress eosinophil counts.
causes_when_low: Allergic conditions, asthma, parasitic infections, or reactions to certain medications can cause eosinophil levels to rise. Elevated cortisol levels (due to stress or steroid use), alcohol intoxication, or certain acute infections can suppress eosinophil counts.
effects_when_high: May point to an active allergic response, asthma flare-up, or a parasitic infection that the immune system is fighting.
effects_when_low: May point to an active allergic response, asthma flare-up, or a parasitic infection that the immune system is fighting.
what_to_do_when_low: Usually requires no direct intervention. If linked to steroid use or high stress, addressing those factors under medical guidance is the best course of action.
what_to_do_when_high: Identify and manage the root trigger - whether it's an allergy, asthma, or an infection. A doctor may recommend allergy testing, antiparasitic treatment, or a review of current medications.

meaning: HbA1c measures the average amount of blood sugar (glucose) attached to hemoglobin over the past three months. Since red blood cells live for approximately 90 days, this test provides a reliable long-term picture of blood sugar control rather than a single point-in-time reading. It is one of the most important markers for diagnosing and monitoring diabetes and pre-diabetes. Unlike fasting glucose, it cannot be influenced by what you ate the day before the test.
causes_when_high: A diet high in refined carbohydrates, sugary drinks, and processed foods is the primary driver of elevated HbA1c, while poor sleep and chronic stress can further raise blood sugar levels even when dietary habits are otherwise reasonable.
causes_when_low: A diet high in refined carbohydrates, sugary drinks, and processed foods is the primary driver of elevated HbA1c, while poor sleep and chronic stress can further raise blood sugar levels even when dietary habits are otherwise reasonable.
effects_when_high: Persistently high HbA1c indicates prolonged elevated blood sugar, which over time can damage blood vessels, nerves, and organs, significantly increasing the risk of diabetes complications, whereas abnormally low levels may suggest excessive blood sugar control or an underlying condition affecting red blood cells.
effects_when_low: Persistently high HbA1c indicates prolonged elevated blood sugar, which over time can damage blood vessels, nerves, and organs, significantly increasing the risk of diabetes complications, whereas abnormally low levels may suggest excessive blood sugar control or an underlying condition affecting red blood cells.
what_to_do_when_low: Abnormally low HbA1c should be discussed with a doctor, particularly if you are on blood sugar-lowering medications, as it may indicate over-management. If not on medication, ensure adequate and balanced carbohydrate intake to maintain stable energy levels.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce intake of refined carbohydrates, sugary drinks, and processed foods. Pairing carbohydrates with protein and fibre helps smooth out blood sugar fluctuations. A short walk of 10–15 minutes after meals, consistent sleep, and staying well hydrated can all contribute to meaningful improvement over 2–3 months.

meaning: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin essential for calcium absorption and bone health. It also supports immune function, muscle function, and helps regulate inflammation and cell growth. It is primarily produced by the body through sun exposure.
causes_when_high: Low levels are commonly caused by limited sun exposure, darker skin tone, high body fat, or a diet lacking in Vitamin D-rich foods. High levels are almost always due to excessive supplementation rather than sun or diet.
causes_when_low: Low levels are commonly caused by limited sun exposure, darker skin tone, high body fat, or a diet lacking in Vitamin D-rich foods. High levels are almost always due to excessive supplementation rather than sun or diet.
effects_when_high: Low Vitamin D can lead to weak bones, poor immunity, and impaired neuromuscular function. Excessively high levels, though rare, can disrupt calcium balance in the body, potentially affecting kidneys and other organs.
effects_when_low: Low Vitamin D can lead to weak bones, poor immunity, and impaired neuromuscular function. Excessively high levels, though rare, can disrupt calcium balance in the body, potentially affecting kidneys and other organs.
what_to_do_when_low: Get sensible sun exposure when possible and consider Vitamin D3 supplementation (preferred over D2), ideally taken with a fat-containing meal for better absorption. Levels typically improve over 4–8 weeks.
what_to_do_when_high: Discontinue or reduce Vitamin D supplements and consult your doctor to reassess dosage.

meaning: Transferrin saturation shows what percentage of the body's iron-carrying capacity is currently being used. It is a reliable indicator of how well iron stores are being utilized and transported through the blood. Low saturation points to iron deficiency, while very high saturation can signal iron overload.
