causes_when_high: Iron deficiency lowers transferrin saturation as there is less iron available to fill binding sites, while iron overload, excess supplementation, or certain genetic conditions can raise it significantly. Recent meals and inflammation can also temporarily affect the reading.
causes_when_low: Iron deficiency lowers transferrin saturation as there is less iron available to fill binding sites, while iron overload, excess supplementation, or certain genetic conditions can raise it significantly. Recent meals and inflammation can also temporarily affect the reading.
effects_when_high: Low transferrin saturation indicates that the body's iron transport system is underutilized due to insufficient iron, which can impair oxygen delivery and energy production, whereas very high saturation suggests excess iron is circulating, which over time can cause oxidative stress and organ damage.
effects_when_low: Low transferrin saturation indicates that the body's iron transport system is underutilized due to insufficient iron, which can impair oxygen delivery and energy production, whereas very high saturation suggests excess iron is circulating, which over time can cause oxidative stress and organ damage.
what_to_do_when_low: Address the underlying iron deficiency through iron-rich dietary sources or supplementation as recommended by a doctor. Saturation levels typically improve gradually over weeks to months as overall iron status normalizes.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to rule out iron overload conditions, particularly if saturation is significantly elevated. Avoid unnecessary iron supplementation and investigate any dietary or genetic factors contributing to excess iron accumulation.

meaning: TSH is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland that signals the thyroid to produce T4 and T3. It acts as the body's thyroid regulator - when thyroid hormone levels drop, TSH rises to stimulate more production, and vice versa. It is typically the first marker tested to assess overall thyroid function.
causes_when_high: TSH usually indicates an underactive thyroid, where the pituitary works harder to compensate for insufficient hormone production. Low TSH suggests an overactive thyroid or excess thyroid hormone from supplementation. Iodine, selenium, and zinc deficiencies, along with chronic stress, poor sleep, and certain medications, can also shift TSH levels.
causes_when_low: TSH usually indicates an underactive thyroid, where the pituitary works harder to compensate for insufficient hormone production. Low TSH suggests an overactive thyroid or excess thyroid hormone from supplementation. Iodine, selenium, and zinc deficiencies, along with chronic stress, poor sleep, and certain medications, can also shift TSH levels.
effects_when_high: Elevated TSH is associated with fatigue, weight gain, brain fog, and sluggishness typical of an underactive thyroid. Low TSH can cause restlessness, rapid heartbeat, weight loss, and anxiety, reflecting an overstimulated thyroid state.
effects_when_low: Elevated TSH is associated with fatigue, weight gain, brain fog, and sluggishness typical of an underactive thyroid. Low TSH can cause restlessness, rapid heartbeat, weight loss, and anxiety, reflecting an overstimulated thyroid state.
what_to_do_when_low: Seek medical evaluation to assess for hyperthyroidism or over-supplementation. Ensure adequate iodine and selenium intake, manage stress, and prioritise sleep to support healthy thyroid signalling.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to evaluate for hypothyroidism. Treatment may involve thyroid hormone replacement, and retesting is recommended after 6–8 weeks of any treatment change.

meaning: Blood urea measures the amount of urea - the body's principal waste product from protein breakdown - circulating in the blood. It is one of the most widely used markers for evaluating how effectively the kidneys are filtering and removing waste.
causes_when_high: Poor kidney function, dehydration, high protein intake, gastrointestinal bleeding, heart failure reducing kidney blood flow, and certain medications can all raise urea levels, while low protein intake or liver dysfunction can cause levels to fall below normal.
causes_when_low: Poor kidney function, dehydration, high protein intake, gastrointestinal bleeding, heart failure reducing kidney blood flow, and certain medications can all raise urea levels, while low protein intake or liver dysfunction can cause levels to fall below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently elevated urea indicates that the kidneys are not clearing waste efficiently, which can lead to a toxic buildup in the body over time, whereas abnormally low urea may reflect inadequate protein intake or reduced liver capacity to produce urea from protein metabolism.
effects_when_low: Persistently elevated urea indicates that the kidneys are not clearing waste efficiently, which can lead to a toxic buildup in the body over time, whereas abnormally low urea may reflect inadequate protein intake or reduced liver capacity to produce urea from protein metabolism.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure sufficient protein intake through a balanced diet. If low urea is accompanied by other abnormal markers, consult a doctor to assess liver function and overall metabolic health.
what_to_do_when_high: Maintain adequate hydration and moderate protein intake to avoid unnecessary urea production. Protecting kidney health through blood pressure control and avoiding harmful medications is equally important.

meaning: The SGOT/SGPT ratio compares the levels of two liver enzymes to help identify the likely cause and source of liver stress. It is a diagnostic tool used alongside individual enzyme levels - the pattern of the ratio can help distinguish between alcohol-related liver damage, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, or damage originating outside the liver such as from the heart or muscles.
causes_when_high: A high ratio (typically above 2) is strongly associated with alcohol-related liver disease or cirrhosis. A low ratio is more commonly seen in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease or hepatitis, where SGPT tends to rise more than SGOT. Muscle or heart injury can also elevate the ratio by raising SGOT independently of liver disease.
causes_when_low: A high ratio (typically above 2) is strongly associated with alcohol-related liver disease or cirrhosis. A low ratio is more commonly seen in non-alcoholic fatty liver disease or hepatitis, where SGPT tends to rise more than SGOT. Muscle or heart injury can also elevate the ratio by raising SGOT independently of liver disease.
effects_when_high: An abnormal ratio in either direction signals liver stress or damage, and the specific pattern helps guide diagnosis. Left unaddressed, the underlying conditions driving the imbalance can progress to more serious liver disease over time.
effects_when_low: An abnormal ratio in either direction signals liver stress or damage, and the specific pattern helps guide diagnosis. Left unaddressed, the underlying conditions driving the imbalance can progress to more serious liver disease over time.
what_to_do_when_low: A low ratio alongside elevated liver enzymes warrants a doctor's evaluation for fatty liver disease or hepatitis. Focus on weight management, a balanced diet, and limiting alcohol to support liver recovery.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce or eliminate alcohol consumption as a priority. Consult a doctor to rule out cirrhosis, heart conditions, or muscle-related causes of elevated SGOT.

meaning: DHT is a potent androgen hormone derived from testosterone through the action of an enzyme called 5-alpha reductase. It plays a key role in the development of male physical characteristics, prostate health, and hair follicle activity, and is present in both men and women, though at significantly higher levels in men.
causes_when_high: Excess testosterone conversion, obesity, insulin resistance, high stress, and certain genetic predispositions can drive DHT levels higher, while 5-alpha reductase inhibitor medications, low testosterone levels, and certain hormonal disorders can suppress DHT below normal.
causes_when_low: Excess testosterone conversion, obesity, insulin resistance, high stress, and certain genetic predispositions can drive DHT levels higher, while 5-alpha reductase inhibitor medications, low testosterone levels, and certain hormonal disorders can suppress DHT below normal.
effects_when_high: Elevated DHT is strongly associated with male pattern baldness, excessive body hair, acne, and prostate enlargement, whereas low DHT can affect the development of male characteristics, reduce libido, and in some cases impair prostate and sexual function.
effects_when_low: Elevated DHT is strongly associated with male pattern baldness, excessive body hair, acne, and prostate enlargement, whereas low DHT can affect the development of male characteristics, reduce libido, and in some cases impair prostate and sexual function.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor to assess overall androgen status and identify the root cause, as treatment depends on whether low DHT is linked to low testosterone, medication use, or an underlying hormonal condition.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to evaluate the underlying hormonal imbalance, and address contributing factors such as obesity or insulin resistance; medications like finasteride may be considered under medical supervision if symptoms are significant.

meaning: Free PSA measures the portion of prostate-specific antigen that circulates in the blood unbound to other proteins. It is used alongside total PSA to help distinguish between prostate cancer and non-cancerous prostate conditions, with the ratio of free to total PSA being particularly useful in guiding further diagnostic decisions.
causes_when_high: A higher proportion of free PSA is generally associated with benign prostate conditions such as enlargement or inflammation, while a lower free PSA ratio relative to total PSA raises the likelihood of prostate cancer being present.
causes_when_low: A higher proportion of free PSA is generally associated with benign prostate conditions such as enlargement or inflammation, while a lower free PSA ratio relative to total PSA raises the likelihood of prostate cancer being present.
effects_when_high: A high free PSA ratio is typically reassuring and suggests a lower probability of malignancy, whereas a low free PSA ratio increases the clinical suspicion for prostate cancer and may prompt further investigation such as a biopsy.
effects_when_low: A high free PSA ratio is typically reassuring and suggests a lower probability of malignancy, whereas a low free PSA ratio increases the clinical suspicion for prostate cancer and may prompt further investigation such as a biopsy.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor promptly for further evaluation, as a low free PSA ratio alongside elevated total PSA may warrant a prostate biopsy or imaging to rule out malignancy.
what_to_do_when_high: A high free PSA ratio is generally a positive finding — continue routine prostate health monitoring as recommended by your doctor based on age and risk factors.

meaning: AFP is a protein primarily produced by the developing liver of a fetus, and its levels in amniotic fluid are used to screen for certain fetal abnormalities during pregnancy. It serves as a key prenatal marker, with abnormal levels pointing to potential neural tube defects, chromosomal conditions, or abdominal wall abnormalities in the developing baby.
causes_when_high: Elevated AFP in amniotic fluid is commonly associated with open neural tube defects like spina bifida, abdominal wall defects, or fetal distress, while unusually low levels may be linked to chromosomal abnormalities such as Down syndrome or other genetic conditions affecting normal fetal development.
causes_when_low: Elevated AFP in amniotic fluid is commonly associated with open neural tube defects like spina bifida, abdominal wall defects, or fetal distress, while unusually low levels may be linked to chromosomal abnormalities such as Down syndrome or other genetic conditions affecting normal fetal development.
effects_when_high: High AFP levels signal a greater likelihood of structural or neural defects in the fetus that may require further diagnostic evaluation and medical planning, whereas low levels may indicate chromosomal issues that can affect the baby's development and long-term health outcomes.
effects_when_low: High AFP levels signal a greater likelihood of structural or neural defects in the fetus that may require further diagnostic evaluation and medical planning, whereas low levels may indicate chromosomal issues that can affect the baby's development and long-term health outcomes.
what_to_do_when_low: Discuss findings with your doctor or a genetic counselor to assess the likelihood of chromosomal conditions and determine whether additional testing is needed for a clearer diagnosis.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a specialist promptly for further diagnostic testing such as detailed ultrasound or amniocentesis to confirm or rule out fetal abnormalities and guide appropriate medical management.

meaning: Potassium is a vital electrolyte that conducts electrical signals within cells and organs, making it essential for normal muscle, nerve, and heart function. It is one of the most tightly regulated minerals in the body, with the kidneys playing the primary role in maintaining its balance.
causes_when_high: Poor kidney function, certain medications like diuretics and ACE inhibitors, and excessive supplementation can disrupt potassium levels, while diarrhea, vomiting, and excessive sweating can cause significant losses that push levels below normal.
causes_when_low: Poor kidney function, certain medications like diuretics and ACE inhibitors, and excessive supplementation can disrupt potassium levels, while diarrhea, vomiting, and excessive sweating can cause significant losses that push levels below normal.
effects_when_high: High potassium can cause dangerous irregular heart rhythms, muscle weakness, and in severe cases, cardiac arrest, whereas low potassium leads to muscle cramps, fatigue, constipation, and abnormal heart rhythms that can also become life-threatening if left unaddressed.
effects_when_low: High potassium can cause dangerous irregular heart rhythms, muscle weakness, and in severe cases, cardiac arrest, whereas low potassium leads to muscle cramps, fatigue, constipation, and abnormal heart rhythms that can also become life-threatening if left unaddressed.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase intake of potassium-rich foods such as bananas, potatoes, and leafy greens. If losses are linked to medications or gastrointestinal issues, addressing those root causes is equally important.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly as significantly elevated potassium can be a medical emergency. Review current medications with your doctor and reduce intake of potassium-rich foods temporarily until levels normalize.

meaning: Red blood cells are the most abundant cells in the blood, responsible for transporting oxygen from the lungs to every tissue in the body and carrying carbon dioxide back for removal. Their count is a direct reflection of the body's oxygen-carrying capacity and is one of the most fundamental markers for assessing overall blood and metabolic health.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, high altitude living, lung conditions, and excess red blood cell production can raise RBC count, while iron, B12, or folate deficiency, bone marrow disorders, chronic disease, and significant blood loss can lower it below normal.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, high altitude living, lung conditions, and excess red blood cell production can raise RBC count, while iron, B12, or folate deficiency, bone marrow disorders, chronic disease, and significant blood loss can lower it below normal.
effects_when_high: A high RBC count can thicken the blood and increase the risk of clotting and circulation problems, whereas a low count reduces oxygen delivery to tissues, causing fatigue, breathlessness, and reduced physical and cognitive performance.
effects_when_low: A high RBC count can thicken the blood and increase the risk of clotting and circulation problems, whereas a low count reduces oxygen delivery to tissues, causing fatigue, breathlessness, and reduced physical and cognitive performance.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate intake of iron, B12, and folate through diet or supplementation as advised by a doctor, with meaningful improvement typically expected over 2–3 months.
what_to_do_when_high: Stay well hydrated and consult a doctor if levels remain elevated to rule out underlying bone marrow or cardiopulmonary conditions driving excess production.

meaning: ALP is an enzyme found primarily in the liver, bones, kidneys, and digestive system, and its blood levels reflect the health and activity of these organs. It is most commonly used as a marker for liver and bone disorders, and is typically assessed alongside other liver enzymes for a more complete picture of organ health.
causes_when_high: Liver disease, bile duct obstruction, bone disorders, rapid bone growth during puberty, certain medications, and thyroid conditions can raise ALP levels, while malnutrition, zinc or magnesium deficiency, hypothyroidism, and certain rare genetic conditions can cause levels to fall below normal.
causes_when_low: Liver disease, bile duct obstruction, bone disorders, rapid bone growth during puberty, certain medications, and thyroid conditions can raise ALP levels, while malnutrition, zinc or magnesium deficiency, hypothyroidism, and certain rare genetic conditions can cause levels to fall below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently elevated ALP suggests active liver stress, bile flow obstruction, or increased bone turnover that warrants further investigation, whereas abnormally low ALP may indicate nutritional deficiencies or underlying conditions affecting bone and liver metabolism.
effects_when_low: Persistently elevated ALP suggests active liver stress, bile flow obstruction, or increased bone turnover that warrants further investigation, whereas abnormally low ALP may indicate nutritional deficiencies or underlying conditions affecting bone and liver metabolism.
what_to_do_when_low: Address any identified nutritional deficiencies such as zinc or magnesium through dietary improvements or supplementation, and consult a doctor if low ALP is accompanied by other abnormal markers suggesting an underlying condition.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to identify the source of elevation — whether liver, bone, or another organ — as further targeted testing is needed to confirm the underlying cause and guide appropriate treatment.

meaning: Total protein measures the combined amount of albumin and globulin — the two major protein groups — circulating in the blood. It provides a broad overview of nutritional status, liver function, kidney health, and immune activity, as both proteins play essential roles in maintaining fluid balance, transporting nutrients, and supporting the immune system.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, chronic infections, autoimmune conditions, and certain inflammatory disorders can raise total protein levels, while malnutrition, poor protein intake, liver disease, kidney disorders causing protein loss, and malabsorption conditions can cause levels to fall below normal.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, chronic infections, autoimmune conditions, and certain inflammatory disorders can raise total protein levels, while malnutrition, poor protein intake, liver disease, kidney disorders causing protein loss, and malabsorption conditions can cause levels to fall below normal.
effects_when_high: Elevated total protein can indicate chronic inflammation, immune system overactivity, or dehydration that is concentrating blood proteins, whereas low total protein reflects poor nutritional status or organ dysfunction that can lead to fluid imbalances, weakened immunity, and impaired tissue repair.
effects_when_low: Elevated total protein can indicate chronic inflammation, immune system overactivity, or dehydration that is concentrating blood proteins, whereas low total protein reflects poor nutritional status or organ dysfunction that can lead to fluid imbalances, weakened immunity, and impaired tissue repair.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate protein intake through a balanced diet rich in eggs, meat, legumes, and dairy, and consult a doctor if low levels persist alongside other abnormal markers suggesting liver, kidney, or absorption issues.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to identify the underlying cause, particularly if chronic infection or an autoimmune condition is suspected, as total protein elevation on its own does not pinpoint a specific diagnosis.

meaning: Globulins are a group of proteins produced by the liver and the immune system that play a vital role in fighting infections, transporting nutrients and hormones, and supporting blood clotting. Their levels in the blood provide useful insight into immune system activity, liver function, and the presence of chronic inflammatory or autoimmune conditions.
causes_when_high: Chronic infections, autoimmune disorders, liver disease, and certain blood cancers like multiple myeloma can elevate globulin levels, while malnutrition, immune deficiency disorders, kidney disease causing protein loss, and certain genetic conditions can cause levels to drop below normal.
causes_when_low: Chronic infections, autoimmune disorders, liver disease, and certain blood cancers like multiple myeloma can elevate globulin levels, while malnutrition, immune deficiency disorders, kidney disease causing protein loss, and certain genetic conditions can cause levels to drop below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently high globulin levels suggest that the immune system is overactive or that chronic inflammation or a serious underlying condition is present, whereas low globulin points to a weakened immune response or poor protein production, leaving the body less equipped to fight infections and maintain fluid balance.
effects_when_low: Persistently high globulin levels suggest that the immune system is overactive or that chronic inflammation or a serious underlying condition is present, whereas low globulin points to a weakened immune response or poor protein production, leaving the body less equipped to fight infections and maintain fluid balance.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate protein intake through a balanced and varied diet, and consult a doctor to rule out immune deficiency or kidney-related protein loss if levels remain consistently low.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor for further evaluation to identify the underlying cause, as persistently elevated globulin alongside other abnormal markers may require targeted testing for autoimmune, liver, or blood-related conditions.

meaning: Fasting blood sugar measures the level of glucose in the blood after a minimum 8-hour fast, providing a clean baseline reading of how well the body regulates blood sugar without the influence of recent food intake. It is one of the primary markers used to screen for and monitor diabetes and pre-diabetes, and is best interpreted alongside HbA1c for a more complete picture of long-term blood sugar control.
causes_when_high: Poor insulin function, high refined carbohydrate and sugar intake, physical inactivity, obesity, chronic stress, and poor sleep can raise fasting glucose, while excessive insulin, prolonged fasting, certain medications, or underlying hormonal conditions can push levels below normal.
causes_when_low: Poor insulin function, high refined carbohydrate and sugar intake, physical inactivity, obesity, chronic stress, and poor sleep can raise fasting glucose, while excessive insulin, prolonged fasting, certain medications, or underlying hormonal conditions can push levels below normal.
effects_when_high: Persistently high fasting glucose indicates impaired blood sugar regulation that over time damages blood vessels and nerves, significantly increasing the risk of diabetes and its complications, whereas low fasting glucose can cause dizziness, shakiness, confusion, and in severe cases loss of consciousness.
effects_when_low: Persistently high fasting glucose indicates impaired blood sugar regulation that over time damages blood vessels and nerves, significantly increasing the risk of diabetes and its complications, whereas low fasting glucose can cause dizziness, shakiness, confusion, and in severe cases loss of consciousness.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure regular balanced meals with adequate carbohydrates and consult a doctor if episodes of low blood sugar occur frequently, as this may indicate an underlying condition requiring investigation.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce refined carbohydrate and sugar intake, incorporate regular physical activity, improve sleep quality, and consult a doctor if levels consistently fall in the pre-diabetic or diabetic range.x

meaning: RDW-CV is a percentage-based measure of the variation in red blood cell size, expressing how inconsistent the cells are relative to their average size. It is the most commonly reported form of RDW and serves as an early indicator of nutritional deficiencies, anemia, or chronic disease affecting red blood cell production, and is best interpreted alongside MCV and hemoglobin for a fuller picture.
causes_when_high: Iron, B12, or folate deficiencies, recent blood loss, transfusions, and chronic inflammatory conditions cause greater size variation and raise RDW-CV, while a consistently uniform red blood cell size and good nutritional status keep it within the normal range.
causes_when_low: Iron, B12, or folate deficiencies, recent blood loss, transfusions, and chronic inflammatory conditions cause greater size variation and raise RDW-CV, while a consistently uniform red blood cell size and good nutritional status keep it within the normal range.
effects_when_high: A high RDW-CV indicates that red blood cells are being produced inconsistently, reducing their collective efficiency in oxygen transport and signaling an underlying nutritional or chronic condition that needs attention, whereas a low or normal RDW-CV reflects healthy and uniform red blood cell production with no cause for concern.
effects_when_low: A high RDW-CV indicates that red blood cells are being produced inconsistently, reducing their collective efficiency in oxygen transport and signaling an underlying nutritional or chronic condition that needs attention, whereas a low or normal RDW-CV reflects healthy and uniform red blood cell production with no cause for concern.
what_to_do_when_low: A low RDW-CV is a positive finding requiring no intervention — simply maintain a nutrient-rich balanced diet to sustain healthy red blood cell uniformity.
what_to_do_when_high: Identify and correct the underlying nutritional deficiency — iron, B12, or folate — through diet or supplementation under medical guidance, and address any chronic inflammation contributing to uneven cell production.

meaning: RCC measures the total number of red blood cells circulating in the blood and directly reflects the body's capacity to transport oxygen to tissues and remove carbon dioxide. It is a foundational marker for diagnosing anemia, polycythemia, and assessing overall blood health, and is typically interpreted alongside hemoglobin and haematocrit for a more complete picture.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, high altitude living, lung conditions, and excess red blood cell production can raise RCC, while iron, B12, or folate deficiency, bone marrow disorders, chronic disease, and significant blood loss can lower it below normal.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, high altitude living, lung conditions, and excess red blood cell production can raise RCC, while iron, B12, or folate deficiency, bone marrow disorders, chronic disease, and significant blood loss can lower it below normal.
effects_when_high: A high RCC can thicken the blood and increase the risk of clotting and circulation problems, whereas a low RCC reduces oxygen delivery to tissues, causing fatigue, breathlessness, and reduced physical and cognitive performance.
effects_when_low: A high RCC can thicken the blood and increase the risk of clotting and circulation problems, whereas a low RCC reduces oxygen delivery to tissues, causing fatigue, breathlessness, and reduced physical and cognitive performance.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure adequate intake of iron, B12, and folate through diet or supplementation as advised by a doctor, with meaningful improvement typically expected over 2–3 months.
what_to_do_when_high: Stay well hydrated and consult a doctor if levels remain elevated to rule out underlying bone marrow or cardiopulmonary conditions driving excess red blood cell production.

meaning: The GK Index is a calculated ratio of blood glucose to ketone levels, used to assess the body's current metabolic state — particularly how effectively it is utilizing fat for fuel versus glucose. It is primarily used by individuals following ketogenic diets or those managing metabolic conditions, with a lower value indicating deeper fat-burning metabolism and better metabolic flexibility.
causes_when_high: High carbohydrate intake, elevated blood sugar, and insulin resistance push the GK Index higher, while strict carbohydrate restriction, fasting, prolonged exercise, and a well-maintained ketogenic diet lower it by raising ketone levels relative to glucose.
causes_when_low: High carbohydrate intake, elevated blood sugar, and insulin resistance push the GK Index higher, while strict carbohydrate restriction, fasting, prolonged exercise, and a well-maintained ketogenic diet lower it by raising ketone levels relative to glucose.
effects_when_high: A high GK Index indicates the body is predominantly relying on glucose for energy with minimal ketone production, whereas a low index reflects a ketotic metabolic state where fat is the primary fuel source, which may offer benefits for metabolic health and certain therapeutic conditions.
effects_when_low: A high GK Index indicates the body is predominantly relying on glucose for energy with minimal ketone production, whereas a low index reflects a ketotic metabolic state where fat is the primary fuel source, which may offer benefits for metabolic health and certain therapeutic conditions.
what_to_do_when_low: A low GK Index is the desired outcome for those following a ketogenic or metabolic therapy protocol — maintain consistent dietary habits and monitor regularly to stay within the target range.
what_to_do_when_high: Reduce carbohydrate and sugar intake, consider intermittent fasting or increased physical activity to shift the body toward fat metabolism, and consult a doctor if metabolic conditions like insulin resistance are suspected.

meaning: Urine pH measures the acidity or alkalinity of urine, reflecting how well the kidneys are maintaining the body's overall acid-base balance. It is a simple but useful marker that can provide early clues about dietary patterns, hydration status, kidney function, and the risk of certain types of kidney stones.
causes_when_high: A high protein and meat-heavy diet, dehydration, uncontrolled diabetes, and certain kidney or respiratory conditions can make urine more acidic and lower pH, while a vegetable-rich diet, urinary tract infections caused by certain bacteria, kidney tubular disorders, and certain medications can raise urine pH making it more alkaline.
causes_when_low: A high protein and meat-heavy diet, dehydration, uncontrolled diabetes, and certain kidney or respiratory conditions can make urine more acidic and lower pH, while a vegetable-rich diet, urinary tract infections caused by certain bacteria, kidney tubular disorders, and certain medications can raise urine pH making it more alkaline.
effects_when_high: Persistently acidic urine increases the risk of uric acid and calcium oxalate kidney stone formation and may reflect an underlying metabolic imbalance, whereas persistently alkaline urine can promote the formation of different types of kidney stones such as struvite and may indicate a urinary tract infection or kidney dysfunction.
effects_when_low: Persistently acidic urine increases the risk of uric acid and calcium oxalate kidney stone formation and may reflect an underlying metabolic imbalance, whereas persistently alkaline urine can promote the formation of different types of kidney stones such as struvite and may indicate a urinary tract infection or kidney dysfunction.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase hydration, moderate high protein and processed food intake, and incorporate more fruits and vegetables into the diet to support a healthier acid-base balance over time.
what_to_do_when_high: Increase fluid intake, review dietary habits particularly around excess vegetable or antacid use, and consult a doctor if persistently alkaline urine is accompanied by other urinary symptoms that may suggest an infection or kidney issue.

meaning: Urobilinogen is a colorless byproduct formed in the intestines during the breakdown of bilirubin — a waste product from the natural recycling of red blood cells. Small amounts are normally present in urine, making it a useful marker for assessing liver function, bile flow, and red blood cell breakdown, with abnormal levels pointing to potential liver, bile duct, or blood disorders.
causes_when_high: Liver disease, hemolytic anemia where red blood cells are broken down excessively, and certain infections can raise urobilinogen levels in urine, while bile duct obstruction blocking bilirubin from reaching the intestines, antibiotic use disrupting gut bacteria, or kidney dysfunction can cause levels to fall below normal.
causes_when_low: Liver disease, hemolytic anemia where red blood cells are broken down excessively, and certain infections can raise urobilinogen levels in urine, while bile duct obstruction blocking bilirubin from reaching the intestines, antibiotic use disrupting gut bacteria, or kidney dysfunction can cause levels to fall below normal.
effects_when_high: Elevated urobilinogen suggests the liver is under stress or that red blood cells are being broken down at an accelerated rate, both of which warrant further investigation, whereas very low or absent urobilinogen may indicate a bile duct blockage that is preventing normal bilirubin processing and requires prompt medical attention.
effects_when_low: Elevated urobilinogen suggests the liver is under stress or that red blood cells are being broken down at an accelerated rate, both of which warrant further investigation, whereas very low or absent urobilinogen may indicate a bile duct blockage that is preventing normal bilirubin processing and requires prompt medical attention.
what_to_do_when_low: Seek medical evaluation to rule out bile duct obstruction or other conditions affecting bilirubin flow, as absent urobilinogen alongside other abnormal markers can indicate a serious underlying condition.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor for further liver and blood evaluation to identify whether the elevation is linked to liver disease, hemolytic conditions, or another underlying cause requiring targeted treatment.

meaning: Urine colour is a simple but informative visual indicator of hydration status, kidney function, and overall health. Normal urine ranges from pale yellow to amber depending on hydration levels, with the colour being determined by a pigment called urochrome. Significant deviations from the normal yellow spectrum can signal underlying health conditions, dietary influences, or medication effects worth investigating.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, certain medications, B vitamin supplementation, liver or bile duct conditions, and the presence of blood or infection can cause urine to appear darker or change colour significantly, while excessive fluid intake or certain medications can produce very pale or nearly colourless urine reflecting over-hydration or reduced kidney concentration ability.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, certain medications, B vitamin supplementation, liver or bile duct conditions, and the presence of blood or infection can cause urine to appear darker or change colour significantly, while excessive fluid intake or certain medications can produce very pale or nearly colourless urine reflecting over-hydration or reduced kidney concentration ability.
effects_when_high: Persistently dark or abnormally coloured urine may signal dehydration, liver dysfunction, blood in the urine, or an active infection that requires medical attention, whereas consistently colourless urine may indicate over-hydration or an impaired ability of the kidneys to concentrate urine properly.
effects_when_low: Persistently dark or abnormally coloured urine may signal dehydration, liver dysfunction, blood in the urine, or an active infection that requires medical attention, whereas consistently colourless urine may indicate over-hydration or an impaired ability of the kidneys to concentrate urine properly.
what_to_do_when_low: Increase fluid intake if dark yellow or amber urine is the concern, and consult a doctor promptly if urine appears red, brown, or cloudy as these colours may indicate blood, liver issues, or infection requiring immediate evaluation.
what_to_do_when_high: If urine is consistently colourless, moderate fluid intake to avoid over-hydration and consult a doctor if this persists alongside frequent urination or other symptoms suggesting a kidney or hormonal issue.

meaning: Urine glucose measures the presence and amount of sugar in the urine, which under normal circumstances should be absent or negligible as healthy kidneys reabsorb virtually all glucose back into the bloodstream. Its presence in urine is most commonly an indicator of elevated blood sugar levels exceeding the kidney's reabsorption threshold, making it a useful screening marker for diabetes and kidney function.
causes_when_high: Uncontrolled or undiagnosed diabetes, gestational diabetes, and certain kidney disorders that impair glucose reabsorption can cause glucose to spill into the urine, while normal kidney function and well-controlled blood sugar levels keep urine glucose absent or at negligible levels.
causes_when_low: Uncontrolled or undiagnosed diabetes, gestational diabetes, and certain kidney disorders that impair glucose reabsorption can cause glucose to spill into the urine, while normal kidney function and well-controlled blood sugar levels keep urine glucose absent or at negligible levels.
effects_when_high: The presence of glucose in urine signals that blood sugar levels are significantly elevated or that the kidneys are not reabsorbing glucose effectively, both of which require medical investigation, whereas absent urine glucose is the normal and desired finding reflecting healthy blood sugar regulation and kidney function.
effects_when_low: The presence of glucose in urine signals that blood sugar levels are significantly elevated or that the kidneys are not reabsorbing glucose effectively, both of which require medical investigation, whereas absent urine glucose is the normal and desired finding reflecting healthy blood sugar regulation and kidney function.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent or negligible urine glucose is a positive finding requiring no intervention — maintaining a balanced diet and healthy lifestyle is sufficient to keep blood sugar within normal limits.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly to assess blood sugar levels and kidney function, as persistent glucose in urine is a strong indicator of diabetes or a renal condition that requires timely diagnosis and management.

meaning: The presence of blood in urine, also known as hematuria, is detected through a urine test and can indicate a range of conditions affecting the urinary tract, kidneys, or bladder. While trace amounts can occasionally appear due to benign causes, any significant or persistent detection of blood in urine warrants medical investigation as it is not a normal finding.
causes_when_high: Urinary tract infections, kidney stones, bladder or kidney injury, strenuous exercise, certain medications, and in more serious cases bladder or kidney cancer can cause blood to appear in urine, while a normal healthy urinary tract with no infection, injury, or underlying condition will produce urine that is free of blood.
causes_when_low: Urinary tract infections, kidney stones, bladder or kidney injury, strenuous exercise, certain medications, and in more serious cases bladder or kidney cancer can cause blood to appear in urine, while a normal healthy urinary tract with no infection, injury, or underlying condition will produce urine that is free of blood.
effects_when_high: The presence of blood in urine can indicate active infection, inflammation, injury, or a more serious underlying condition affecting the kidneys or urinary tract that requires prompt evaluation, whereas the absence of blood in urine is the normal and desired finding reflecting a healthy urinary system.
effects_when_low: The presence of blood in urine can indicate active infection, inflammation, injury, or a more serious underlying condition affecting the kidneys or urinary tract that requires prompt evaluation, whereas the absence of blood in urine is the normal and desired finding reflecting a healthy urinary system.
what_to_do_when_low: No blood in urine is a reassuring and normal finding — maintain good hydration and urinary hygiene to support a healthy urinary tract.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly as blood in urine should never be ignored — further testing such as urine culture, imaging, or cystoscopy may be needed to identify and treat the underlying cause.

meaning: Pus cells, also known as white blood cells in urine or pyuria, are detected during a urine examination and indicate that the immune system is actively responding to an infection or inflammation somewhere in the urinary tract. Their presence is not normal and even a small number above the acceptable threshold is considered a significant finding that warrants further investigation.
causes_when_high: Urinary tract infections, kidney infections, sexually transmitted infections, kidney stones causing irritation, and in some cases bladder or kidney inflammation can cause pus cells to appear in urine, while a healthy urinary tract free of infection or inflammation will have negligible or no pus cells present.
causes_when_low: Urinary tract infections, kidney infections, sexually transmitted infections, kidney stones causing irritation, and in some cases bladder or kidney inflammation can cause pus cells to appear in urine, while a healthy urinary tract free of infection or inflammation will have negligible or no pus cells present.
effects_when_high: Elevated pus cells in urine indicate an active infection or inflammatory response in the urinary tract that if left untreated can progress to more serious kidney or systemic complications, whereas absent or negligible pus cells reflect a healthy urinary tract with no signs of infection or inflammation.
effects_when_low: Elevated pus cells in urine indicate an active infection or inflammatory response in the urinary tract that if left untreated can progress to more serious kidney or systemic complications, whereas absent or negligible pus cells reflect a healthy urinary tract with no signs of infection or inflammation.
what_to_do_when_low: No or negligible pus cells is a normal and reassuring finding — maintain adequate hydration and good urinary hygiene to support continued urinary tract health.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for a urine culture to identify the causative organism and receive appropriate antibiotic or anti-inflammatory treatment before the infection spreads or worsens.

meaning: Epithelial cells are cells that line the urinary tract, bladder, and kidneys, and small numbers are normally shed into the urine as part of routine cell turnover. Their presence in urine is assessed during a microscopic examination, and while a few cells are considered normal, elevated numbers can indicate infection, inflammation, or in some cases kidney disease depending on the type of epithelial cell detected.
causes_when_high: Urinary tract infections, kidney disease, contamination during sample collection, bladder inflammation, and certain kidney disorders can cause elevated epithelial cells in urine, while a clean and properly collected urine sample from a healthy urinary tract will contain only minimal epithelial cells within the acceptable range.
causes_when_low: Urinary tract infections, kidney disease, contamination during sample collection, bladder inflammation, and certain kidney disorders can cause elevated epithelial cells in urine, while a clean and properly collected urine sample from a healthy urinary tract will contain only minimal epithelial cells within the acceptable range.
effects_when_high: A significantly elevated epithelial cell count suggests active irritation, infection, or damage along the urinary tract or kidneys that warrants further investigation, whereas a low or normal count reflects healthy cell turnover with no signs of underlying urinary tract pathology.
effects_when_low: A significantly elevated epithelial cell count suggests active irritation, infection, or damage along the urinary tract or kidneys that warrants further investigation, whereas a low or normal count reflects healthy cell turnover with no signs of underlying urinary tract pathology.
what_to_do_when_low: A low or normal epithelial cell count is a reassuring finding requiring no intervention — maintaining good hydration and urinary hygiene is sufficient to support ongoing urinary tract health.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor to determine the type of epithelial cells present and identify the underlying cause, as different cell types point to different areas of concern within the urinary tract or kidneys requiring targeted evaluation.

meaning: Urine crystals are formed when certain minerals and chemicals in the urine become concentrated and solidify into crystalline structures. While small amounts of some crystal types can be normal depending on diet and hydration, their presence in significant numbers or in certain forms can indicate an increased risk of kidney stone formation, metabolic imbalances, or underlying kidney and liver conditions.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, high intake of purine-rich foods, excess oxalate or calcium in the diet, urinary tract infections, certain metabolic disorders, and liver disease can promote crystal formation and raise their count in urine, while adequate hydration and a balanced diet typically keep crystal formation minimal or absent.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, high intake of purine-rich foods, excess oxalate or calcium in the diet, urinary tract infections, certain metabolic disorders, and liver disease can promote crystal formation and raise their count in urine, while adequate hydration and a balanced diet typically keep crystal formation minimal or absent.
effects_when_high: A significant presence of urine crystals indicates an elevated risk of kidney stone formation and may point to an underlying metabolic or dietary imbalance that needs to be addressed, whereas absent or minimal crystals reflect healthy urine composition with a low risk of stone formation.
effects_when_low: A significant presence of urine crystals indicates an elevated risk of kidney stone formation and may point to an underlying metabolic or dietary imbalance that needs to be addressed, whereas absent or minimal crystals reflect healthy urine composition with a low risk of stone formation.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent or minimal crystals is a normal and positive finding — maintaining adequate hydration and a balanced diet is sufficient to keep urine composition healthy and crystal formation at bay.
what_to_do_when_high: Increase fluid intake significantly to dilute urine concentration, adjust dietary habits based on the type of crystals identified, and consult a doctor to rule out metabolic disorders or an increased risk of kidney stones requiring further management.

meaning: Nitrites in urine are a chemical byproduct produced when certain bacteria convert nitrates — naturally present in urine from dietary sources — into nitrites. Their presence is a reliable indirect indicator of a bacterial urinary tract infection, as healthy urine does not normally contain nitrites, making this a quick and useful screening marker for detecting bacterial activity in the urinary tract.
causes_when_high: Bacterial urinary tract infections caused by organisms such as E. coli and other nitrate-reducing bacteria are the primary cause of positive nitrite results, while a healthy infection-free urinary tract, insufficient urine retention time in the bladder, or infections caused by bacteria that do not convert nitrates will produce a negative or absent nitrite result.
causes_when_low: Bacterial urinary tract infections caused by organisms such as E. coli and other nitrate-reducing bacteria are the primary cause of positive nitrite results, while a healthy infection-free urinary tract, insufficient urine retention time in the bladder, or infections caused by bacteria that do not convert nitrates will produce a negative or absent nitrite result.
effects_when_high: A positive nitrite result strongly suggests an active bacterial infection in the urinary tract that if left untreated can progress to a kidney infection or more serious systemic complications, whereas a negative nitrite result in the absence of other abnormal urine findings is reassuring and reflects a healthy urinary environment.
effects_when_low: A positive nitrite result strongly suggests an active bacterial infection in the urinary tract that if left untreated can progress to a kidney infection or more serious systemic complications, whereas a negative nitrite result in the absence of other abnormal urine findings is reassuring and reflects a healthy urinary environment.
what_to_do_when_low: A negative nitrite result is a normal and reassuring finding — maintain good hydration and urinary hygiene to support a healthy and infection-free urinary tract.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for a urine culture to confirm the infection and identify the causative bacteria, and begin appropriate antibiotic treatment to prevent the infection from spreading to the kidneys.

meaning: Urine ketones are chemical compounds produced when the body breaks down fat for energy in the absence of sufficient glucose, and their presence in urine indicates that the body has shifted into a fat-burning metabolic state. While small amounts can appear during fasting or low carbohydrate diets, significantly elevated levels can signal a serious metabolic imbalance particularly in individuals with diabetes, making it an important marker to monitor.
causes_when_high: Prolonged fasting, strict low carbohydrate or ketogenic diets, uncontrolled diabetes particularly type 1, intense prolonged exercise, and conditions causing poor carbohydrate utilization can raise urine ketone levels, while adequate carbohydrate intake and well-controlled blood sugar keep ketone production minimal and urine ketones absent or negligible.
causes_when_low: Prolonged fasting, strict low carbohydrate or ketogenic diets, uncontrolled diabetes particularly type 1, intense prolonged exercise, and conditions causing poor carbohydrate utilization can raise urine ketone levels, while adequate carbohydrate intake and well-controlled blood sugar keep ketone production minimal and urine ketones absent or negligible.
effects_when_high: Mildly elevated ketones in a non-diabetic individual following a ketogenic diet are generally not harmful, however significantly elevated ketones particularly in diabetics can indicate diabetic ketoacidosis — a serious and potentially life-threatening condition requiring immediate medical attention, whereas absent ketones reflect normal glucose metabolism with no excessive fat breakdown occurring.
effects_when_low: Mildly elevated ketones in a non-diabetic individual following a ketogenic diet are generally not harmful, however significantly elevated ketones particularly in diabetics can indicate diabetic ketoacidosis — a serious and potentially life-threatening condition requiring immediate medical attention, whereas absent ketones reflect normal glucose metabolism with no excessive fat breakdown occurring.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent or negligible urine ketones is the normal finding for most individuals — maintaining a balanced diet with adequate carbohydrates and good blood sugar control is sufficient to keep ketone production within a healthy range.
what_to_do_when_high: Diabetic individuals with elevated urine ketones should seek immediate medical attention to rule out diabetic ketoacidosis, while non-diabetic individuals should ensure adequate hydration and carbohydrate intake and consult a doctor if ketones remain persistently elevated without a dietary explanation.

meaning: Urine casts are cylindrical structures formed in the tiny tubules of the kidneys when proteins, cells, or other materials clump together and take the shape of the tubule before being flushed into the urine. Their presence and type provide direct insight into kidney health, with different cast types pointing to specific kidney conditions ranging from mild stress to serious kidney disease.
causes_when_high: Kidney infections, acute or chronic kidney disease, dehydration, strenuous exercise, autoimmune conditions affecting the kidneys, and certain medications can cause casts to appear in urine, while healthy kidneys with normal tubular function will produce urine that is largely free of pathological casts.
causes_when_low: Kidney infections, acute or chronic kidney disease, dehydration, strenuous exercise, autoimmune conditions affecting the kidneys, and certain medications can cause casts to appear in urine, while healthy kidneys with normal tubular function will produce urine that is largely free of pathological casts.
effects_when_high: The presence of casts — particularly cellular or granular types — signals active kidney stress, inflammation, or damage that requires prompt medical evaluation, whereas hyaline casts in small numbers can occasionally appear after intense exercise or dehydration and are generally not a cause for concern on their own.
effects_when_low: The presence of casts — particularly cellular or granular types — signals active kidney stress, inflammation, or damage that requires prompt medical evaluation, whereas hyaline casts in small numbers can occasionally appear after intense exercise or dehydration and are generally not a cause for concern on their own.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent or minimal hyaline casts is a normal finding requiring no intervention — maintaining good hydration and kidney health through a balanced diet and healthy blood pressure is sufficient.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for further kidney function testing, as the type of cast present is critical in determining the severity and nature of any underlying kidney condition requiring targeted treatment.

meaning: Urine protein measures the amount of protein present in the urine, which under normal circumstances should be absent or at very low levels as healthy kidneys retain virtually all proteins within the bloodstream. Its presence in significant amounts is a key indicator of kidney dysfunction, as it reflects a breakdown in the kidney's filtering mechanism that is allowing proteins to leak through into the urine.
causes_when_high: Kidney disease, uncontrolled diabetes, high blood pressure, urinary tract infections, strenuous exercise, fever, dehydration, and certain autoimmune conditions can cause protein to spill into the urine, while healthy kidney function and well-controlled blood pressure and blood sugar keep urine protein absent or within negligible limits.
causes_when_low: Kidney disease, uncontrolled diabetes, high blood pressure, urinary tract infections, strenuous exercise, fever, dehydration, and certain autoimmune conditions can cause protein to spill into the urine, while healthy kidney function and well-controlled blood pressure and blood sugar keep urine protein absent or within negligible limits.
effects_when_high: Persistently elevated urine protein signals progressive kidney damage that if left unaddressed can accelerate the decline of kidney function and increase the risk of cardiovascular disease over time, whereas absent or minimal urine protein reflects healthy kidney filtration with no signs of significant protein leakage.
effects_when_low: Persistently elevated urine protein signals progressive kidney damage that if left unaddressed can accelerate the decline of kidney function and increase the risk of cardiovascular disease over time, whereas absent or minimal urine protein reflects healthy kidney filtration with no signs of significant protein leakage.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent or negligible urine protein is a normal and reassuring finding — maintaining healthy blood pressure, blood sugar control, and adequate hydration is sufficient to support continued kidney health.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for further kidney function evaluation including eGFR and creatinine testing, and address any underlying conditions such as diabetes or high blood pressure that may be driving kidney stress and protein leakage.

meaning: The presence of bacteria in urine, detected during a microscopic urine examination, indicates that microorganisms have entered the urinary tract and may be causing or have the potential to cause an infection. While urine is normally sterile, small amounts of bacteria can sometimes appear due to sample contamination, making proper sample collection critical for an accurate result.
causes_when_high: Urinary tract infections, poor genital hygiene, catheter use, kidney infections, sexually transmitted infections, and improper urine sample collection leading to contamination can all cause bacteria to appear in urine, while a properly collected sample from a healthy urinary tract will be free of any significant bacterial presence.
causes_when_low: Urinary tract infections, poor genital hygiene, catheter use, kidney infections, sexually transmitted infections, and improper urine sample collection leading to contamination can all cause bacteria to appear in urine, while a properly collected sample from a healthy urinary tract will be free of any significant bacterial presence.
effects_when_high: A significant bacterial presence in urine indicates an active or developing urinary tract infection that if left untreated can ascend to the kidneys and cause more serious complications, whereas absent bacteria in a properly collected sample confirms a healthy and infection-free urinary tract.
effects_when_low: A significant bacterial presence in urine indicates an active or developing urinary tract infection that if left untreated can ascend to the kidneys and cause more serious complications, whereas absent bacteria in a properly collected sample confirms a healthy and infection-free urinary tract.
what_to_do_when_low: No bacteria in urine is a normal and reassuring finding — maintain good hydration, proper genital hygiene, and healthy lifestyle habits to support continued urinary tract health.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for a urine culture to identify the specific bacteria and receive appropriate antibiotic treatment, while increasing fluid intake to help flush the urinary tract during recovery.

meaning: Leucocyte esterase is an enzyme released by white blood cells, and its detection in urine indicates that white blood cells are present in the urinary tract as part of an immune response. It is a reliable and widely used screening marker for urinary tract infections and inflammation, as white blood cells are not normally found in significant amounts in healthy urine.
causes_when_high: Urinary tract infections, kidney infections, sexually transmitted infections, bladder inflammation, kidney stones causing irritation, and in some cases contamination during sample collection can cause a positive leucocyte esterase result, while a healthy infection-free urinary tract will produce a negative result with no significant white blood cell activity present.
causes_when_low: Urinary tract infections, kidney infections, sexually transmitted infections, bladder inflammation, kidney stones causing irritation, and in some cases contamination during sample collection can cause a positive leucocyte esterase result, while a healthy infection-free urinary tract will produce a negative result with no significant white blood cell activity present.
effects_when_high: A positive leucocyte esterase result indicates active immune activity in the urinary tract pointing to an infection or inflammation that if left untreated can progress to more serious kidney or systemic complications, whereas a negative result in the absence of other abnormal findings is reassuring and reflects a healthy urinary environment.
effects_when_low: A positive leucocyte esterase result indicates active immune activity in the urinary tract pointing to an infection or inflammation that if left untreated can progress to more serious kidney or systemic complications, whereas a negative result in the absence of other abnormal findings is reassuring and reflects a healthy urinary environment.
what_to_do_when_low: A negative leucocyte esterase result is a normal and reassuring finding — maintain good hydration, proper genital hygiene, and a healthy lifestyle to support continued urinary tract health.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for further evaluation including a urine culture to confirm infection and identify the causative organism, and begin appropriate treatment to prevent the condition from worsening or spreading.

meaning: Yeast cells in urine are detected during a microscopic urine examination and indicate the presence of a fungal organism — most commonly Candida — in the urinary tract. While yeast is not normally found in urine, its presence can point to a fungal urinary tract infection, genital yeast infection causing contamination during sample collection, or an underlying condition that has compromised the body's natural defenses.
causes_when_high: Diabetes, prolonged antibiotic use disrupting normal bacterial flora, a weakened immune system, catheter use, and poor genital hygiene can promote yeast overgrowth and cause yeast cells to appear in urine, while a healthy immune system and balanced microbial environment typically prevent yeast from establishing in the urinary tract.
causes_when_low: Diabetes, prolonged antibiotic use disrupting normal bacterial flora, a weakened immune system, catheter use, and poor genital hygiene can promote yeast overgrowth and cause yeast cells to appear in urine, while a healthy immune system and balanced microbial environment typically prevent yeast from establishing in the urinary tract.
effects_when_high: A significant yeast cell presence indicates an active fungal infection or overgrowth that if left untreated can cause persistent discomfort, recurring infections, and in immunocompromised individuals may spread beyond the urinary tract, whereas absent yeast cells reflect a healthy urinary environment free of fungal activity.
effects_when_low: A significant yeast cell presence indicates an active fungal infection or overgrowth that if left untreated can cause persistent discomfort, recurring infections, and in immunocompromised individuals may spread beyond the urinary tract, whereas absent yeast cells reflect a healthy urinary environment free of fungal activity.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent yeast cells is a normal and reassuring finding — maintaining good hygiene, a balanced diet low in excess sugar, and a healthy immune system is sufficient to prevent yeast overgrowth.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor for appropriate antifungal treatment and to identify any underlying condition such as uncontrolled diabetes or immune suppression that may be promoting yeast overgrowth in the urinary tract.

meaning: Bile pigments in urine — primarily bilirubin — are waste products formed during the breakdown of red blood cells that are normally processed by the liver and excreted through bile into the digestive tract. Their presence in urine is abnormal and serves as a direct indicator of liver dysfunction, bile duct obstruction, or conditions causing excessive red blood cell breakdown, making it a useful early marker for detecting liver and biliary system disorders.
causes_when_high: Liver disease such as hepatitis or cirrhosis, bile duct obstruction caused by gallstones or tumors, and hemolytic conditions where red blood cells are broken down excessively can cause bile pigments to appear in urine, while healthy liver function and normal bile flow will keep urine completely free of detectable bile pigments.
causes_when_low: Liver disease such as hepatitis or cirrhosis, bile duct obstruction caused by gallstones or tumors, and hemolytic conditions where red blood cells are broken down excessively can cause bile pigments to appear in urine, while healthy liver function and normal bile flow will keep urine completely free of detectable bile pigments.
effects_when_high: The presence of bile pigments in urine signals that the liver is under significant stress or that bile is not flowing normally, which if left unaddressed can indicate progressive liver damage or a serious biliary obstruction requiring prompt medical attention, whereas absent bile pigments reflect normal liver function and healthy bile processing with no cause for concern.
effects_when_low: The presence of bile pigments in urine signals that the liver is under significant stress or that bile is not flowing normally, which if left unaddressed can indicate progressive liver damage or a serious biliary obstruction requiring prompt medical attention, whereas absent bile pigments reflect normal liver function and healthy bile processing with no cause for concern.
what_to_do_when_low: Absent bile pigments in urine is the normal and desired finding — maintaining a liver-friendly lifestyle through a balanced diet, adequate hydration, limited alcohol intake, and healthy body weight is sufficient to support ongoing liver health.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for further liver function testing and imaging to identify the underlying cause, as the presence of bile pigments in urine is always an abnormal finding that requires timely investigation and appropriate medical management.

meaning: Urine transparency refers to the clarity or cloudiness of a urine sample and is assessed as part of a routine urine examination. Normal urine is typically clear to slightly hazy, and significant cloudiness or turbidity can indicate the presence of infection, excess minerals, proteins, or other substances that do not normally appear in healthy urine in significant amounts.
causes_when_high: Urinary tract infections, excess protein, kidney stones, the presence of pus cells, bacteria, yeast, or crystals, and poor hydration can all cause urine to appear cloudy or turbid, while adequate hydration and a healthy urinary tract free of infection or excess mineral concentration will produce clear and transparent urine.
causes_when_low: Urinary tract infections, excess protein, kidney stones, the presence of pus cells, bacteria, yeast, or crystals, and poor hydration can all cause urine to appear cloudy or turbid, while adequate hydration and a healthy urinary tract free of infection or excess mineral concentration will produce clear and transparent urine.
effects_when_high: Persistently cloudy or turbid urine suggests an underlying condition such as infection, kidney stress, or mineral imbalance that warrants further investigation, whereas clear and transparent urine is the normal and desired finding reflecting good hydration and a healthy urinary tract.
effects_when_low: Persistently cloudy or turbid urine suggests an underlying condition such as infection, kidney stress, or mineral imbalance that warrants further investigation, whereas clear and transparent urine is the normal and desired finding reflecting good hydration and a healthy urinary tract.
what_to_do_when_low: Clear and transparent urine requires no intervention — maintaining adequate hydration and a balanced diet is sufficient to support healthy urine composition and clarity.
what_to_do_when_high: Increase fluid intake and consult a doctor if cloudiness persists or is accompanied by other abnormal urine findings, as further testing may be needed to identify and treat the underlying cause such as infection or kidney stone formation.

meaning: PT INR measures how long it takes for blood to clot by evaluating the function of the clotting factors produced by the liver, and expresses the result as a standardized ratio for consistency across different laboratories. It is a critical marker for assessing bleeding risk, monitoring patients on blood-thinning medications like warfarin, and evaluating overall liver function and clotting factor production.
causes_when_high: Liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, blood-thinning medications, and certain clotting factor deficiencies can prolong clotting time and raise the INR, while excessive vitamin K intake, certain medications, or hypercoagulable conditions that accelerate clotting can lower it below the normal range.
causes_when_low: Liver disease, vitamin K deficiency, blood-thinning medications, and certain clotting factor deficiencies can prolong clotting time and raise the INR, while excessive vitamin K intake, certain medications, or hypercoagulable conditions that accelerate clotting can lower it below the normal range.
effects_when_high: A high INR indicates that blood is taking longer than normal to clot, significantly increasing the risk of uncontrolled bleeding even from minor injuries, whereas a low INR suggests the blood is clotting too quickly, raising the risk of dangerous clot formation in blood vessels that can lead to stroke or thrombosis.
effects_when_low: A high INR indicates that blood is taking longer than normal to clot, significantly increasing the risk of uncontrolled bleeding even from minor injuries, whereas a low INR suggests the blood is clotting too quickly, raising the risk of dangerous clot formation in blood vessels that can lead to stroke or thrombosis.
what_to_do_when_low: Consult a doctor to evaluate for hypercoagulable conditions or medication interactions, as an abnormally low INR may indicate an increased clotting risk that requires further investigation and appropriate medical management.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly particularly if on blood-thinning medications, as dose adjustment may be needed, and ensure adequate vitamin K intake through dietary sources such as leafy greens under medical guidance.

meaning: Post prandial blood sugar measures the level of glucose in the blood typically 2 hours after a meal, reflecting how effectively the body processes and clears glucose following food intake. It is an important complement to fasting blood sugar and HbA1c, providing insight into how well insulin is responding to a glucose load and helping identify impaired glucose tolerance that may not be apparent from fasting readings alone.
causes_when_high: High refined carbohydrate and sugar intake, insulin resistance, physical inactivity, poor pancreatic function, chronic stress, and disrupted sleep can cause post meal glucose to remain elevated, while excessive insulin response, certain medications, or insufficient carbohydrate intake during the meal can cause levels to drop below normal after eating.
causes_when_low: High refined carbohydrate and sugar intake, insulin resistance, physical inactivity, poor pancreatic function, chronic stress, and disrupted sleep can cause post meal glucose to remain elevated, while excessive insulin response, certain medications, or insufficient carbohydrate intake during the meal can cause levels to drop below normal after eating.
effects_when_high: Persistently elevated post prandial glucose indicates impaired glucose clearance that over time contributes to blood vessel and nerve damage, increasing the risk of diabetes complications, whereas abnormally low post meal glucose can cause dizziness, shakiness, sweating, and fatigue as the body struggles to maintain stable energy levels after eating.
effects_when_low: Persistently elevated post prandial glucose indicates impaired glucose clearance that over time contributes to blood vessel and nerve damage, increasing the risk of diabetes complications, whereas abnormally low post meal glucose can cause dizziness, shakiness, sweating, and fatigue as the body struggles to maintain stable energy levels after eating.
what_to_do_when_low: Ensure balanced meals with adequate carbohydrates and avoid skipping meals, and consult a doctor if post meal glucose drops are frequent or severe as this may indicate an exaggerated insulin response requiring further evaluation.
what_to_do_when_high: Pair carbohydrates with protein, fiber, and healthy fats to slow glucose absorption, incorporate a short walk after meals, reduce refined carbohydrate portions, and consult a doctor if levels consistently exceed normal post meal thresholds.

meaning: CA 72-4 is a tumor-associated glycoprotein used primarily as a tumor marker for gastric (stomach) cancer, and is also associated with certain ovarian and colorectal cancers. It is most commonly used alongside other tumor markers to monitor treatment response and detect cancer recurrence rather than as a primary diagnostic tool, as elevated levels can occasionally occur in certain benign conditions as well.
causes_when_high: Gastric cancer, ovarian cancer, colorectal cancer, pancreatitis, liver disease, and certain benign gastrointestinal conditions can raise CA 72-4 levels, while healthy individuals with no underlying malignancy or significant gastrointestinal condition will typically maintain levels within the normal low range.
causes_when_low: Gastric cancer, ovarian cancer, colorectal cancer, pancreatitis, liver disease, and certain benign gastrointestinal conditions can raise CA 72-4 levels, while healthy individuals with no underlying malignancy or significant gastrointestinal condition will typically maintain levels within the normal low range.
effects_when_high: Persistently elevated CA 72-4 raises concern for an active malignancy or significant gastrointestinal condition that requires prompt medical investigation, whereas normal or low levels in a monitored patient are generally reassuring and may indicate stable disease or a positive response to treatment.
effects_when_low: Persistently elevated CA 72-4 raises concern for an active malignancy or significant gastrointestinal condition that requires prompt medical investigation, whereas normal or low levels in a monitored patient are generally reassuring and may indicate stable disease or a positive response to treatment.
what_to_do_when_low: Normal or low CA 72-4 is a reassuring finding — continue routine health monitoring and screening as recommended by your doctor based on individual risk factors and medical history.
what_to_do_when_high: Consult a doctor promptly for further diagnostic evaluation including imaging and additional tumor markers to identify the underlying cause, particularly if accompanied by symptoms such as abdominal discomfort, unexplained weight loss, or changes in digestion.

meaning: Electrolytes are essential minerals — primarily sodium, potassium, chloride, and bicarbonate — that carry an electrical charge and are critical for maintaining fluid balance, nerve signaling, muscle function, and the body's acid-base balance. A serum electrolyte panel measures the levels of these key minerals together, providing a broad and important overview of hydration status, kidney function, and overall metabolic health.
causes_when_high: Dehydration, excessive fluid intake, kidney dysfunction, hormonal imbalances, certain medications like diuretics, prolonged vomiting or diarrhea, and poor dietary intake can all disrupt electrolyte balance and push individual or multiple electrolyte levels outside the normal range in either direction.
causes_when_low: Dehydration, excessive fluid intake, kidney dysfunction, hormonal imbalances, certain medications like diuretics, prolonged vomiting or diarrhea, and poor dietary intake can all disrupt electrolyte balance and push individual or multiple electrolyte levels outside the normal range in either direction.
effects_when_high: Electrolyte imbalances can have wide-ranging effects on the body including muscle cramps, weakness, irregular heart rhythms, confusion, and in severe cases life-threatening cardiac or neurological complications, whereas balanced electrolyte levels support healthy nerve conduction, muscle performance, and stable fluid distribution throughout the body.
effects_when_low: Electrolyte imbalances can have wide-ranging effects on the body including muscle cramps, weakness, irregular heart rhythms, confusion, and in severe cases life-threatening cardiac or neurological complications, whereas balanced electrolyte levels support healthy nerve conduction, muscle performance, and stable fluid distribution throughout the body.
what_to_do_when_low: Replenish depleted electrolytes through dietary sources or oral rehydration solutions depending on the specific deficiency, address any underlying cause such as excessive fluid loss or poor intake, and consult a doctor if levels are significantly low or accompanied by symptoms requiring medical intervention.
what_to_do_when_high: Identify and address the specific electrolyte that is elevated, ensure adequate hydration, review current medications with a doctor, and seek medical evaluation if imbalances are significant or persistent as electrolyte disorders can escalate quickly without appropriate management.

meaning: CA 19-9 is a protein that exists on the surface of certain cancer cells and is shed into the bloodstream, making it a useful tumor marker primarily associated with pancreatic, bile duct, and gastrointestinal cancers. It is most commonly used to monitor treatment response and disease progression in diagnosed patients rather than as a standalone screening tool, as elevated levels can also occur in several non-cancerous conditions.
causes_when_high: Pancreatic cancer, bile duct cancer, colorectal cancer, stomach cancer, liver disease, pancreatitis, and bile duct obstruction can all raise CA 19-9 levels, while healthy individuals with no underlying malignancy or inflammatory condition will typically have levels within the normal low range.
causes_when_low: Pancreatic cancer, bile duct cancer, colorectal cancer, stomach cancer, liver disease, pancreatitis, and bile duct obstruction can all raise CA 19-9 levels, while healthy individuals with no underlying malignancy or inflammatory condition will typically have levels within the normal low range.
effects_when_high: Persistently and significantly elevated CA 19-9 raises concern for an underlying malignancy or serious biliary and pancreatic condition that requires prompt medical investigation, whereas normal or low levels in a monitored patient generally indicate stable disease or a positive response to ongoing treatment.
